title: Unpinned third-party GitHub Action receives a secret token
description: The composite action invokes `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` and passes `inputs.oauth_token` into it. A major-version tag is mutable, so a compromised upstream action or retargeted tag could execute attacker-controlled code in CI while receiving the Claude OAuth token. The calling workflow also grants the job pull-request write and OIDC token permissions, increasing impact.
recommendation: Pin the third-party action to a reviewed immutable commit SHA, keep updates automated through Dependabot/Renovate, and continue using least-privilege job permissions.

title: Untrusted PR metadata is injected into an agent prompt with write-capable tools
description: The prompt embeds attacker-controlled PR metadata such as the PR title directly into the instruction context, then gives the Claude agent instructions and allowed tools that include writing a file and posting a PR comment through `gh pr comment`. This is not shell injection, but it is an agent prompt-injection surface: a malicious PR title can try to override the review instructions or cause the agent to post attacker-controlled content. The limited tool allowlist and `pull_request` event reduce impact, but they do not remove the model-level trust boundary issue.
recommendation: Treat GitHub event fields as untrusted data: place them in a clearly delimited JSON/data block after the operational instructions, explicitly instruct the agent not to follow instructions from metadata or diff content, and avoid including PR title/body fields unless needed.

title: Quality gate executes mutable action tags
description: The PR quality gate pulls actions/checkout@v4, pnpm/action-setup@v4, and actions/setup-node@v4 by mutable major-version tags. If an action tag is moved or the publisher is compromised, arbitrary code runs inside a required API Quality Gate and can falsify the status that branch protection trusts. This job has read-only permissions and no secrets, so the impact is CI integrity rather than direct credential theft.
recommendation: Pin every external action to a full commit SHA and use Dependabot or a scheduled workflow to review SHA updates.

title: OTA deployment path uses mutable actions with Expo credentials
description: The workflow uses mutable action tags throughout CI, including the OTA job that later authenticates with EXPO_TOKEN. A compromised checkout, pnpm setup, Node setup, or Expo action tag can modify the workspace, capture the Expo token, or alter the eas update invocation to publish a malicious preview OTA bundle. The main CI path has lower impact, but the same mutable-tag pattern is present there too.
recommendation: Pin external actions to full commit SHAs, especially in the ota-update job, and keep the Expo token scoped to the minimum EAS permissions needed for preview updates.

title: PR-controlled local action receives Claude OAuth secrets
description: On pull_request, actions/checkout defaults to the PR merge commit. The workflow then executes ./.github/actions/claude-review from that checkout and passes CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN_2, and CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN_3 into it. A same-repository PR, or a fork PR if secret policies are relaxed, can modify .github/actions/claude-review/action.yml to run arbitrary code and exfiltrate those tokens. The job also grants id-token: write, so the PR-controlled local action can request OIDC tokens if any cloud trust is configured.
recommendation: Do not pass secrets to local actions loaded from PR-controlled checkouts. Run the review action from the base branch or a pinned reusable workflow, set checkout persist-credentials: false where possible, remove id-token: write unless strictly required, and pin the underlying Anthropic action by SHA.

title: Any @claude issue or comment can invoke a secret-backed agent
description: The job condition only checks whether issue, comment, or review text contains @claude. It does not verify author_association, team membership, labels, or maintainer approval before starting anthropics/claude-code-action with CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and id-token: write. On repos where untrusted users can open issues or comment, an attacker can consume the Claude OAuth quota and drive the agent with prompt content under their control; the unnecessary OIDC permission increases the impact if the action/tooling can run shell commands.
recommendation: Gate invocation to trusted actors, for example OWNER, MEMBER, or COLLABORATOR author associations, or require a maintainer-applied label/approval. Remove id-token: write unless the action truly needs it and configure restrictive Claude tool permissions.

title: Secret-backed Claude workflow uses mutable action tags
description: The workflow runs actions/checkout@v4 and anthropics/claude-code-action@v1 by mutable tags in a job that has id-token: write and passes the Claude OAuth token to the Anthropic action. If either action tag is moved or the publisher is compromised, attacker-controlled code can run with the job permissions and the Claude credential.
recommendation: Pin external actions to full commit SHAs and review updates deliberately. Keep job permissions at contents: read only unless a specific action step needs more.

title: Deploy can mark new migrations as applied without running them
description: The deploy job runs packages/database/scripts/baseline-migrations.mjs immediately before drizzle-kit migrate. That helper inserts every missing hash from the current migration journal into drizzle.__drizzle_migrations whenever an accounts table exists. On any future deploy containing a new migration, the baseline step records the new hash first, so Drizzle will skip the SQL and deploy code against a database that never received the new columns, constraints, or data rewrites.
recommendation: Make the baseline script one-time/manual only, or only allow it when the migration journal is empty and a fixed cutoff migration is provided. Remove this step from normal deploys after reconciliation and add a regression test proving a newly added migration is not baselined before migrate runs.

title: Deployment secrets are exposed to an unpinned curl-to-shell installer
description: The Doppler sync step exports CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and DOPPLER_TOKEN, then executes curl -sL https://cli.doppler.com/install.sh | sh in the same shell step. If the remote install script, CDN, DNS, or TLS path is compromised, arbitrary code runs with both deployment credentials and can exfiltrate secrets or modify Worker configuration.
recommendation: Install Doppler in a separate step before secrets are in the environment, using a pinned package version with checksum/signature verification or a prebuilt trusted runner image. Avoid curl-to-shell in secret-bearing deploy jobs.

title: Deploy and mobile build jobs use mutable action tags with deployment credentials
description: The deploy workflow uses mutable major tags for checkout, pnpm setup, Node setup, github-script, and Expo actions. In the API deploy and mobile build jobs, a compromised action can persist changes into later steps that run migrations, deploy Workers, sync secrets, or authenticate with EXPO_TOKEN; expo/expo-github-action@v8 directly receives the Expo token.
recommendation: Pin all external actions to full commit SHAs, reduce workflow-level issues: write to only the notification jobs, and keep deployment secrets scoped to the exact steps that need them.

title: Staging smoke checks accept missing protected routes
description: The smoke-test comments say /v1/auth/me and /v1/sessions should return 401 and not 404, but the shell checks only fail on 5xx responses. A staging deploy where either protected route is missing or not mounted would return 404 and still pass the smoke test, masking a broken API rollout.
recommendation: Make these smoke checks assert the expected 401 status, or explicitly allow only a documented small set of acceptable non-5xx statuses. Fail on 404 and unexpected 2xx responses.

title: Privileged workflow_run executes pull-request code with seed secret access
description: The workflow is triggered by completed CI runs, and the upstream CI workflow also runs on pull_request. This workflow explicitly handles pull_request workflow_run events, checks out github.event.workflow_run.head_sha, and the mobile-maestro job executes the checked-out API/mobile code. In the same job it writes secrets.TEST_SEED_SECRET into apps/api/.dev.vars before starting wrangler dev and running build/test tooling. A malicious PR that passes unprivileged CI and touches mobile-relevant files can then run attacker-controlled Worker, Expo, Gradle, or Maestro code in this privileged workflow and read or exfiltrate the seed secret from the workspace.
recommendation: Do not run secret-bearing workflow_run jobs on PR heads. Gate these jobs with github.event.workflow_run.event == 'push', github.event.workflow_run.head_branch == 'main', and github.event.workflow_run.head_repository.full_name == github.repository before checkout or secret use. Run PR E2E without secrets, or require explicit trusted approval and avoid checking out untrusted code in the privileged job.

title: Remote Maestro installer is piped to bash after secrets are written to the workspace
description: The job writes TEST_SEED_SECRET into apps/api/.dev.vars, then later installs Maestro using curl -Ls https://get.maestro.mobile.dev | bash and runs an unpinned third-party emulator action. If the installer endpoint, DNS/TLS delivery, or third-party action tag is compromised, arbitrary code runs on a runner whose workspace contains the seed secret file.
recommendation: Install Maestro from a pinned versioned artifact and verify a checksum or signature before execution. Pin third-party actions to full commit SHAs, and run any unavoidable remote installers before secrets are materialized or in an isolated job without secret-bearing files.

title: Pull-request workflow runs checked-out PR code with Clerk and test seed secrets
description: The workflow runs on pull_request, places TEST_SEED_SECRET and CLERK_SECRET_KEY in the job environment, checks out the PR code, then runs pnpm install and the Playwright smoke suite from that checkout. If secrets are available to the PR context, such as same-repository PR branches, private-fork settings, or a compromised collaborator account, a malicious package lifecycle script, Playwright config, helper, or test can read and exfiltrate the Clerk secret key and test seed secret. GitHub's default secret withholding only protects external fork PRs; it is not a workflow-level guard for all PR sources.
recommendation: Do not expose secrets to pull_request jobs that execute checked-out PR code. Split untrusted PR smoke tests into a no-secret workflow, and run staging/Clerk-backed tests only after merge, on trusted manual dispatch, or behind a protected environment with explicit approval. Avoid package lifecycle execution in any secret-bearing PR job.

title: Secondary seed cleanup uses a fallback prefix that does not match the Playwright run
description: The reset step tries to derive PREFIX from PLAYWRIGHT_EMAIL_PREFIX or PLAYWRIGHT_RUN_ID, but PLAYWRIGHT_RUN_ID is set from a workflow env value that is never defined. The actual run id and seed email prefix are generated inside the Playwright Node process in apps/mobile/e2e-web/helpers/runtime.ts, and that process cannot export its mutated environment back to this later shell step. As a result, the secondary cleanup falls back to pw-fallback- and will not reset accounts seeded with the real pw-playwright-<timestamp>-... prefix when globalTeardown is skipped or fails.
recommendation: Define PLAYWRIGHT_RUN_ID or PLAYWRIGHT_EMAIL_PREFIX at the job level before running Playwright, for example from github.run_id and github.run_attempt, and reuse that exact value in the cleanup step. Prefer calling the existing reset helper from Node so the prefix is URL-encoded consistently.

title: Mobile build workflow_run is not explicitly restricted to push events from this repository
description: The workflow is intended to build after CI passes on main, but the job conditions only require a successful workflow_run and affected/native-change outputs. They do not verify that the triggering CI run was a push from this repository. The branches: [main] filter is not a sufficient in-job authorization boundary for workflow_run events involving pull-request CI. A PR workflow_run that matches the branch filter and touches native mobile files can drive the privileged build path and consume EAS build capacity with EXPO_TOKEN before any merge.
recommendation: Add an explicit guard before any build path: github.event.workflow_run.event == 'push' && github.event.workflow_run.head_branch == 'main' && github.event.workflow_run.head_repository.full_name == github.repository. Alternatively replace this with a direct push-to-main trigger for the automated build job and keep workflow_dispatch separate.

title: Unpinned Expo GitHub Action receives the EAS token
description: The workflow uses expo/expo-github-action@v8 and passes it secrets.EXPO_TOKEN in both automated and manual build paths. Because the action is pinned only to a major tag, a compromised or retagged upstream action release could execute arbitrary code with the EAS token available.
recommendation: Pin expo/expo-github-action to a full commit SHA and use Dependabot or a scheduled review process to update it. Keep the job permissions minimal and consider protected environments for production-profile builds.

title: Resend webhook is mounted behind Clerk auth
description: index.ts applies authMiddleware globally before registering routes, then mounts resendWebhookRoute. The Resend handler is POST /webhooks/resend and is designed to be unauthenticated at the Clerk layer, with its own Svix signature verification, but authMiddleware's public-path list does not include /v1/webhooks/resend. A real Resend/Svix webhook will not carry a Clerk Bearer JWT, so the global auth middleware rejects it with 401 before the route can verify the Svix signature or process bounce/complaint events. This can permanently break email delivery observability and suppression workflows in production.
recommendation: Add /v1/webhooks/resend to the auth public-path allowlist in apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts, and add an integration test through the full app proving POST /v1/webhooks/resend reaches the route without Clerk auth while still requiring valid Svix headers.

title: LLM-derived ask-gate reason is logged as free text
description: The observer schema accepts an arbitrary reason string and writes it directly to structured logs. The producer passes evaluateSessionDepth().reason; for LLM-gated sessions that value is model output derived from the learner transcript. A learner can include sensitive text or prompt-inject the model to echo transcript details into the reason, causing child/session content to be retained in Cloudflare logs. The schema-drift branch also logs rawData on invalid payloads.
recommendation: Log stable reason codes and method names instead of LLM free text. If free-text diagnostics are required, cap length aggressively, scrub/redact before logging, and make the producer map LLM explanations to an enum before dispatch.

title: Reminder emails reuse consent tokens after they expire
description: The reminder workflow fetches the current consentToken by profileId and sends direct approval links at day 7 and day 14. The token created by requestConsent expires after 7 days, and processConsentResponse rejects expired tokens, so the day-7 reminder is race-prone and the day-14 reminder link is definitely expired. A parent following the reminder can be unable to approve consent, and the profile can later be auto-deleted despite the reminder claiming there are days remaining.
recommendation: Refresh the consent token and expiresAt before sending each reminder, or make the token lifetime match the full consent window. Add a test that a day-14 reminder link can still be accepted by processConsentResponse.

title: Older consent reminder runs can delete after a newer request
description: The Inngest event is keyed only by profileId, while requestConsent can upsert a new token/requestedAt and dispatch another app/consent.requested event for the same profile. The delayed day-30 step from an older run only checks that the profile is not CONSENTED or WITHDRAWN before calling deleteProfileIfNoConsent. If consent is resent near the end of the old 30-day window, the older run can delete the profile before the newer request's window has elapsed.
recommendation: Include a consent state id, requestedAt, or generation/version in the event payload. Before every delayed reminder and especially before deletion, re-read the latest consent row and bail if it does not match the event generation.

title: Consent restoration can race with final archive or delete
description: The worker checks restoration once after the 7-day sleep, then performs more steps before the final mutation. The hard-delete path does not recheck consent immediately before deleteProfile(), so a restore after check-restoration can still be deleted. The archive path rechecks status before updating archivedAt, but that check and the update are not atomic; restoreConsent can set CONSENTED and clear archivedAt between them, after which this worker can set archivedAt again. That leaves a restored profile deleted or indefinitely archived.
recommendation: Make the final archive/delete operation atomic and conditional on the current consent state still being WITHDRAWN. For example, update/delete with a WHERE EXISTS consent_states status='WITHDRAWN' guard, return the affected row count, and treat zero rows as restored/no-op. Alternatively lock the consent/profile rows in one transaction before the final mutation.

title: Queued feedback retry drops the original submission
description: The retry worker can only read profileId and category from the event schema, and the retry email body contains only a delayed-delivery placeholder with the category/profile. The producer in routes/feedback.ts dispatches the same minimal payload when the original sendEmail call fails, while the API returns success with queued:true. As a result, the user's actual feedback message and client metadata are silently lost whenever the first email attempt fails; the retry can never deliver the original support request.
recommendation: Put a durable retry payload behind the event: either include the bounded feedback message and metadata in app/feedback.delivery_failed, or persist the submission server-side and send a submission id. Do not report queued:true unless the worker can reconstruct and deliver the original feedback. Include a unique event id or submission id in the idempotency key fallback.

title: Memory embedding backfill ignores memory consent before external processing
description: The cron selects every memory_facts row with embedding IS NULL and sends row.text to generateEmbedding/Voyage without joining learning_profiles or profiles to confirm memoryConsentStatus is still granted, collection is still enabled, or the profile is not archived. Other memory write paths explicitly skip analysis unless memoryConsentStatus === 'granted' and memoryCollectionEnabled is true, and grantMemoryConsent('declined') disables memory flags without deleting existing memory_facts. A backlog row created while consent was granted but not embedded, for example because the Voyage key was missing or the provider was rate-limited, would still be sent to the external embedding provider after consent was declined or the profile was archived.
recommendation: Filter the backlog query to eligible profiles by joining learning_profiles and profiles, at minimum requiring memoryConsentStatus = 'granted' and profiles.archived_at IS NULL. Decide whether memoryCollectionEnabled should also gate embedding of existing facts, and add regression tests for declined consent plus pending embedding rows.

title: Monthly report generation does not revalidate current parent-child access
description: `monthlyReportGenerate` trusts `parentId` and `childId` from the queued `app/monthly-report.generate` event. It checks the child's GDPR consent, but it never re-checks that `parentId` still has a current `familyLinks` relationship to `childId` before reading child snapshots/struggles, inserting a parent-scoped report, and sending push/email. Because Inngest events are durable and can retry later, a report event queued while a link existed can still run after that link is removed, leaking child report availability and struggle topics to a former parent.
recommendation: Inside `monthlyReportGenerate`, before child reads or user-visible sends, require either `parentId === childId` for self-reports or a current family-link/access check. Re-check before notification sends if generation and notification remain separate retryable steps.

title: Monthly report push ignores push opt-out
description: The monthly report handler always attempts `sendPushNotification` after only the 24-hour dedup check. The `notificationPreferences` lookup below only gates `monthlyProgressEmail`, and `sendPushNotification`/`getPushToken` only require an existing Expo token, not `pushEnabled`. A parent who disabled push notifications but still has a stored token can still receive a monthly report push containing the child display name. The weekly digest path correctly checks `pushEnabled` before pushing.
recommendation: Load parent notification preferences before the monthly push and require `pushEnabled` plus an explicit monthly push preference, or at minimum `pushEnabled`, before calling `sendPushNotification`.

title: Background LLM suggestion job does not re-check withdrawn consent
description: The function handles an async `app/filing.completed` event outside the API consent middleware path. After verifying the book belongs to `profileId`, it builds an LLM prompt from learner/book/topic data and calls `routeAndCall`, then persists new suggestions. There is no latest GDPR consent check before the LLM call or before the write. If consent is withdrawn after the filing route queues the event, or an old event is replayed, this job can still send learner curriculum data to the LLM provider and create new derived data for a withdrawn profile.
recommendation: After the ownership check, query the latest GDPR consent state for `profileId` and skip unless it is absent/allowed by policy or `CONSENTED`; always skip `WITHDRAWN`. Consider a second check before persisting derived suggestions, and add a regression test for a queued filing event after withdrawal.

title: Parent progress summary generation ignores current child consent
description: The job only checks that the profile has a parent link before loading the child's profile, building a knowledge inventory, generating an LLM summary, and persisting it. It does not check the latest GDPR consent state. A debounced or replayed `app/session.completed` event can therefore process and store parent-facing derived learning data after a child profile has moved to a non-consented or withdrawn state.
recommendation: Before loading inventory or calling the LLM, query the latest GDPR consent state for the child profile and skip when consent is restricted or withdrawn. Re-check before persisting the summary, and add a regression test for withdrawal between session completion and the debounced job run.

title: Child-controlled learning labels can influence parent-facing LLM prose
description: The function generates a free-form parent-facing summary from `generateProgressSummary` and accepts the returned text after trimming. The underlying prompt sanitizes and XML-wraps inventory fields, which helps, but child-controlled subject names still become model input and the output is not constrained by a structured schema or allowlist before being shown to another user. A learner-controlled label can therefore steer or pollute trusted parent-facing app prose.
recommendation: Use structured output with strict validation for the summary, fall back on validation failure, and add allowlist/content checks appropriate for parent-facing progress copy. Add prompt-injection regression fixtures using hostile subject names.

title: Recall nudge topic lookup is not scoped to the recipient profile
description: The handler trusts event.data.topTopicIds and reads curriculumTopics globally by id before putting the first title into a push notification. The normal cron derives topTopicIds from retentionCards for the profile, but retentionCards can contain an independent profileId/topicId pair and the recall-test flow auto-creates cards for a user-supplied topicId without proving that the topic belongs to the profile. If an attacker can obtain another profile's topic UUID, they can create a due card under their own profile and receive that other profile's topic title in a recall nudge.
recommendation: Resolve topic titles through curriculumTopics -> curriculumBooks -> subjects and require subjects.profileId = profileId, or use a scoped repository helper. Also enforce topic ownership before creating retentionCards.

title: Recall nudge scan can notify archived profiles
description: The cross-profile scan starts from profiles and joins retention_cards and notification_preferences, but it never excludes profiles where archived_at is set. The family removal path archives same-account non-owner profiles by setting profiles.archivedAt and deleting familyLinks while leaving retention cards and notification preferences in place. For adult or legacy profiles with no consent rows, the notExists(consentStates) branch still marks the profile eligible, so the cron can fan out app/recall-nudge.send with topic IDs for a removed profile. The consent check also accepts any CONSENTED row across consent types, which can let a stale legacy consent row override a newer non-active GDPR row.
recommendation: Add an isNull(profiles.archivedAt) eligibility condition, and align the consent predicate with the canonical profile consent status or the active GDPR row instead of accepting any CONSENTED row. Add tests covering archived profiles with pushEnabled retention cards and legacy mixed consent rows.

title: Review reminder scan can notify archived profiles
description: The scan joins profiles to retention_cards and notification_preferences but does not filter profiles.archivedAt. Same-account family removal archives non-owner profiles instead of deleting their retention cards or push preferences, so an archived adult or legacy profile with no consent rows can still satisfy the no-consent-record adult branch and be fanned out to app/retention.review-due. That send path formats a push from the supplied overdue count and topic IDs, leaking learning-state information for a profile that has been removed or archived. The consent predicate also treats any CONSENTED row as sufficient, even if another consent type is currently non-active.
recommendation: Require profiles.archivedAt IS NULL in the scan and use the same consent-status semantics as profile-scoped reads, preferably the active GDPR/latest consent state. Add regression tests for archived profiles and mixed legacy consent states.

title: Parent-facing session insights use an unescaped transcript
description: `sessionCompleted` builds `transcriptText` by concatenating raw `sessionEvents.content` and passes it to `generateSessionInsights`. The imported helper wraps that string in a `<transcript>` tag without XML escaping, unlike the recap, vocabulary, and learner-profile analysis helpers. A learner can include a closing tag or fake instructions in the transcript and steer the parent-facing JSON fields (`highlight`, `narrative`, `conversationPrompt`) as long as the output passes the prefix/length/schema checks. This is a cross-user LLM injection path from learner text to parent-visible recap content.
recommendation: Escape each transcript turn before wrapping it, or reuse the existing escaped transcript builder pattern. Also consider input-side injection detection that forces low confidence before accepting parent-facing recap JSON.

title: Curriculum prewarm sends learner data to LLM without async consent revalidation
description: The prewarm job validates the event and verifies the book belongs to the profile, but then loads learner age, calls `generateBookTopics`, and persists generated topics without checking the profile's current consent state. Because `/v1/inngest` is intentionally outside Clerk/consent middleware, a subject-created event queued before withdrawal can still run afterward and process book title/description plus learner age through the LLM.
recommendation: Add a consent gate inside the Inngest function before `generateBookTopics` and skip non-consented/withdrawn profiles. Re-check before persistence if the LLM call may take long, and cover withdrawn-after-dispatch with a test.

title: Curriculum retry can process withdrawn profiles in the background
description: The retry job verifies book/profile ownership, then gets learner age, calls `generateBookTopics`, and writes generated topics without checking the current consent state. Since the event runs through Inngest rather than the API consent middleware, a retry event queued before consent withdrawal can still send learner data to the LLM provider and create derived curriculum after withdrawal.
recommendation: Check the latest GDPR consent state inside the function before any LLM call or derived-data write, skipping at least `PENDING`, `PARENTAL_CONSENT_REQUESTED`, and `WITHDRAWN` according to product policy.

title: Retry curriculum events are not idempotent before an expensive LLM call
description: Unlike the prewarm function, the retry function config has only concurrency limiting and no idempotency key. The authenticated `/subjects/:id/retry-curriculum` path can enqueue retry events for the same stuck book; duplicate events that start before `topicsGenerated` flips can each reach `generateBookTopics`, burning extra LLM calls even though only one result is needed.
recommendation: Add Inngest idempotency keyed by `event.data.bookId`, and/or atomically claim the book generation attempt in the database before calling the LLM. Also rate-limit or meter the retry route that dispatches this event.

title: Topic-probe metadata writes can clobber concurrent session metadata
description: Both the success path and onFailure cleanup read the whole learning_sessions.metadata object, spread it in application code, and write the whole JSON blob back without a row lock, transaction, JSONB patch, or compare-and-swap guard. Any concurrent session metadata writer that commits between the SELECT and UPDATE can be silently erased. The failure cleanup also unconditionally writes topicProbeExtractionStatus:'failed', so a duplicate/replayed failing run can mask a previously completed extraction.
recommendation: Use atomic JSONB updates such as jsonb_set/jsonb_build_object for only the topic-probe keys, or lock the row with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE inside a transaction. For failure cleanup, update only when the current topicProbeFiredAt matches the payload timestamp and the current status is still pending; never downgrade completed to failed.

title: Fan-out suppresses send failures so weekly report batches are not retried
description: `sendBatchedEvents` catches `step.sendEvent` failures, records Sentry, increments `failedBatches`, and returns normally. The cron/backfill callers then return `partial` instead of throwing. Because Inngest sees the function as successful, a transient send failure permanently drops up to 200 report-generation events for that batch; the next weekly cron will move to a different reporting window.
recommendation: Let failed `step.sendEvent` calls fail the function, or throw after recording failed batches so Inngest retries. The stable step labels already give a path to retry without duplicating successful memoized sends. If partial success is required, enqueue failed batches to a durable retry event.

title: Email-only account reclamation can rebind an existing account without an email-verification check
description: accountMiddleware only checks that the JWT contains some email value, then passes it to findOrCreateAccount. The traced service reclaims an existing local account when the email matches but clerkUserId differs, updating that account to the current JWT sub. No code checks an email_verified-style claim or performs a server-side Clerk lookup before this rebind. If Clerk configuration ever issues a session token with an unverified or attacker-controlled duplicate email, the first API request can take over the existing local account, including profiles and billing state.
recommendation: Require a verified primary email signal before provisioning or reclamation. Prefer moving email-based reclaim to a signed Clerk webhook or explicit recovery flow, or validate the current Clerk user server-side and only rebind after confirmed verified email ownership.

title: Resend webhook is unintentionally blocked by Clerk auth
description: PUBLIC_PATHS omits /v1/webhooks/resend even though apps/api/src/routes/resend-webhook.ts declares the route as a public Svix-signed webhook and index.ts mounts it globally. Because authMiddleware runs before routes and only skips the listed paths, Resend webhook deliveries will receive a Clerk 401 before the route can verify svix-* signatures. This breaks bounce/complaint/delivery handling and associated observability.
recommendation: Add the exact /v1/webhooks/resend path to PUBLIC_PATHS and add a negative/positive middleware test proving it bypasses Clerk but still rejects missing or invalid Svix signatures in the route.

title: Broad exempt prefixes bypass consent enforcement for profile data writes
description: The middleware returns immediately for any path matching EXEMPT_PREFIXES before it checks WITHDRAWN, PENDING, or PARENTAL_CONSENT_REQUESTED consent states. The exempt list includes /v1/profiles and /v1/onboarding/, and those route surfaces include personal-data writes such as profile updates, pronouns, language preference, and interests. A profile whose consent is pending or withdrawn can therefore continue submitting data on these exempt prefixes with an otherwise valid authenticated session, bypassing the stated consent-processing gate.
recommendation: Split exemptions by consent state and method. Run the WITHDRAWN block before broad exemptions, and allow only the minimal recovery/consent/deletion/status endpoints for withdrawn profiles. For pending consent, exempt only endpoints strictly required to request/inspect consent, not profile/onboarding personal-data writes. Add negative tests for PATCH /profiles/:id and PATCH /onboarding/* under PENDING and WITHDRAWN states.

title: Production deploy gate can 500 every request when IDEMPOTENCY_KV is not bound
description: The middleware refuses to serve production traffic when validateProductionBindings reports a missing binding, returning a 500 before auth or routing. Static config in apps/api/wrangler.toml still has IDEMPOTENCY_KV commented out for production/staging, while ALLOW_MISSING_IDEMPOTENCY_KV defaults to false in config.ts. If deployed in that state without an out-of-band binding or explicit override, every production request will fail at this middleware.
recommendation: Add the IDEMPOTENCY_KV binding to the production and staging Worker config before enabling this gate, or set an explicit temporary override in Doppler and track it. Add a CI/deploy check that fails when ENVIRONMENT=production and the wrangler config lacks the required KV binding.

title: LLM route allowlist misses authenticated LLM-backed endpoints
description: The metering matcher only covers sessions, quiz generation, dictation, recall-bridge, evaluate-depth, and retry-filing patterns. Static tracing found externally reachable authenticated routes outside this allowlist that call routeAndCall, including /v1/ocr via GeminiOcrProvider.extractText, /v1/filing via fileToLibrary, and subject/curriculum classifier or explanation endpoints. Because nonmatching paths return before quota logic, an authenticated client can repeatedly invoke paid LLM work without quota decrement or a route-level rate limit.
recommendation: Enforce abuse protection at the LLM router boundary or maintain a complete method-aware allowlist for every externally reachable routeAndCall/routeAndStream surface. Add sweep tests that fail when a route/service calling the LLM router is not covered by quota or a documented separate rate limit.

title: Quota is not refunded when a metered handler throws
description: meteringMiddleware decrements quota before await next(), then only refunds if next() returns normally with an HTTP status >= 400. There is no try/catch/finally around await next(), so thrown downstream errors skip the refund path and go to the global error handler. Several metered LLM handlers can throw provider or parse errors without their own refund catch, causing users to lose monthly/daily quota for failed work.
recommendation: Wrap await next() in try/catch and refund on thrown errors before rethrowing, with a per-request flag or idempotent refund marker to avoid double-refunding handlers that already refunded explicitly. Keep the existing status-based refund for normal 4xx/5xx responses.

title: Account deletion can be marked scheduled without a durable deletion job
description: POST /account/delete commits deletionScheduledAt via scheduleDeletion and then queues the only scheduled-deletion Inngest event through safeSend. safeSend is explicitly best-effort: it swallows send failures and timeouts after logging/capturing. The handler still returns 'Deletion scheduled', but if inngest.send failed before acceptance, no app/account.deletion-scheduled event exists and the scheduled-account-deletion function will never sleep and execute the deletion. This is a regulatory-critical data deletion path, not non-core telemetry.
recommendation: Make deletion dispatch durable before returning success. Treat the Inngest send as core and fail/rollback on dispatch failure, or write a transactional outbox/deletion-jobs row that a retrying worker/cron drains independently of the request. Do not use safeSend for the primary deletion job.

title: Assessment creation accepts unverified subject and topic IDs
description: POST /subjects/:subjectId/topics/:topicId/assessments takes both IDs from route params and passes them directly to createAssessment with only the resolved profileId. The service insert binds profileId, but it does not first prove that subjectId belongs to that profile or that topicId belongs to that subject's curriculum. An authenticated caller who learns another profile's subject/topic UUIDs can create assessment, retention, and XP-linked rows under their own profile that reference another tenant's curriculum records. This is the same ownership-chain check that session start performs before accepting a topicId.
recommendation: Before inserting, verify subjectId is owned by the resolved profile and topicId joins through curricula/subjects to that same subject/profile. Reject on mismatch before createAssessment.

title: Assessment LLM calls are not metered and terminal assessments can be replayed
description: The answer and quick-check handlers call evaluateAssessmentAnswer/evaluateQuickCheckAnswer, which route through the LLM router, but metering only covers sessions, quiz, dictation, and filing paths. There is also no status guard before calling the LLM, so a caller can keep POSTing answers to an already-terminal assessment and continue triggering LLM calls while appending history and updating retention/practice activity. A valid session ID likewise allows repeated unmetered quick-check LLM calls.
recommendation: Add assessment answer and quick-check routes to metering or add route-local quota/rate limiting. Reject submissions for terminal assessments before the LLM call, and enforce the exchange cap before evaluation.

title: Billing management routes do not enforce owner-profile authorization
description: Sensitive billing operations use only the authenticated account and do not check that the active resolved profile is the owner/guardian. A non-owner profile in a family account can reach checkout creation, subscription cancellation, top-up PaymentIntent creation, billing portal creation, and family pool/member reads/adds. The remove-family route does check profileMeta.isOwner, which shows the intended authorization boundary is missing from the other billing management surfaces.
recommendation: Gate billing management and full family billing reads behind a server-side owner/guardian check, and return only redacted self-usage data to non-owner profiles. Apply the same guard consistently to checkout, cancel, top-up, portal, family add/list, and similar billing endpoints.

title: Proxy-mode child profile can trigger suggestion-generation writes
description: The top-up path only requires a resolved profileId, then calls getUnpickedBookSuggestionsWithTopup when topup=1. That service updates the subject generation timestamp and can insert bookSuggestions for the resolved profile. Because the route does not call assertNotProxyMode(), a parent/proxy request with a child X-Profile-Id can mutate the child's suggestion state despite proxy sessions being intended read-only for child profiles.
recommendation: Reject proxy mode before running the top-up branch, or move top-up to an explicit POST endpoint guarded by assertNotProxyMode(). Keep the read-only suggestions path available without the write guard.

title: Proxy-mode child profile writes are not blocked
description: The topic-generation and topic-move handlers resolve a server-verified profileId but never call assertNotProxyMode(). Because profileScopeMiddleware allows an authenticated account to select a child profile via X-Profile-Id and marks it as non-owner in profileMeta, a parent/guardian proxy session can mutate the child's curriculum by generating topics or moving topics. Other write routes in the app explicitly call assertNotProxyMode() for this trust boundary.
recommendation: Import and call assertNotProxyMode(c) at the start of every curriculum write handler before performing service calls.

title: Topic move can target a topic outside the authenticated subject
description: The route passes subjectId, source bookId, topicId, and targetBookId into moveTopicToBook(). The service verifies the authenticated profile owns subjectId and that targetBookId belongs to that subject, but its source-topic check only verifies curriculumTopics.id equals topicId and curriculumTopics.bookId equals the supplied sourceBookId. It does not verify that the source book, topic curriculum, or topic owner belongs to the authenticated subject/profile before updating the topic row. If an attacker learns another profile's source book/topic UUIDs, they can issue the move request with their own target book and corrupt the victim's topic row.
recommendation: In moveTopicToBook(), verify the source book belongs to the same authenticated subject and verify the topic through a parent-chain join: topic -> curriculum/book -> subject -> profileId. Include that ownership predicate in the UPDATE WHERE clause.

title: Book topic generation is outside LLM quota enforcement
description: The generate-topics handler calls generateBookTopics(), which routes to the LLM provider, but meteringMiddleware does not include this route in its LLM-consuming route patterns. An authenticated user can trigger paid LLM calls for curriculum generation without decrementing subscription quota or hitting a per-route rate limit.
recommendation: Add this route to the centralized metering/rate-limit policy, or move generation behind a durable job with per-profile idempotency and quota accounting.

title: Failed topic generation permanently marks a book as generated
description: The route first claims a book by setting topicsGenerated=true, then calls the LLM and persists topics. If getProfileAge(), generateBookTopics(), or persistBookTopics() fails after the claim, later calls see the book as already claimed/generated and return the existing book without topics. This can leave curriculum books permanently empty with no retry path from this endpoint.
recommendation: Use a separate generation status/lock field, or set topicsGenerated=true only in the same successful transaction that persists topics. On failure, release the claim or mark it retryable.

title: Client-controlled viewer can mark child celebrations seen in proxy mode
description: POST /celebrations/seen accepts viewer from the request body and writes seen state for the resolved profile without assertNotProxyMode() or a server-derived role check. A parent/proxy request for a child profile can send viewer='child' and update celebrationsSeenByChild, suppressing celebrations in the child's own experience.
recommendation: Derive the allowed viewer from server-side profileMeta/account role. At minimum, reject viewer='child' when profileMeta.isOwner is false, or apply assertNotProxyMode() to the endpoint if proxy sessions should not write celebration state at all.

title: Expired or already-used consent tokens still disclose the child name
description: GET /consent-page and /deny-confirm use getChildNameByToken() to render the child's display name before enforcing token expiry or terminal consent status. processConsentResponse() rejects expired or already-processed tokens later, but the display pages still reveal that the token exists and disclose the child name. The POST path also fetches the name before processing and only catches the exact 'Invalid consent token' message, so expired/already-processed token errors fall through as 500s instead of the intended expired-link response.
recommendation: Replace getChildNameByToken() on public pages with a token validation helper that checks existence, expiry, and non-terminal status before returning profile data. Catch typed consent token errors instead of string-matching one message.

title: Consent denial is not atomic with profile deletion
description: The route delegates denial to processConsentResponse(). That service updates the consent row to WITHDRAWN and then deletes the child profile in a separate operation. If the delete fails after the status update commits, the token becomes terminal and cannot be retried, while the child's profile and learning data can remain undeleted. This is a regulatory-critical deletion path.
recommendation: Make the consent status transition and profile deletion atomic in one database transaction, or transition to a deletion_pending state and enqueue an observable retryable deletion job before making the token terminal.

title: Consent request email sending can be abused by rotating parent emails
description: POST /consent/request sends a Resend email to caller-supplied parentEmail after only verifying the child profile belongs to the authenticated account and that the email is not the account email. The service resend cap is per current parentEmail and resets when the email changes, so an authenticated attacker can rotate recipient addresses to send arbitrary consent emails and consume email-provider capacity. There is no per-account, per-profile, or per-IP rate limit on this email-sending route.
recommendation: Add an account/profile scoped rate limit for consent request sends, cap unique recipient changes over a time window, and keep the existing per-recipient resend limit as a secondary control.

title: Curriculum mutation endpoints omit the proxy-mode write guard
description: The skip, unskip, add topic, challenge/regenerate, and adapt endpoints mutate curriculum state using the verified profileId but never call assertNotProxyMode. The services verify that the subject belongs to that profile, but they do not reject non-owner child profiles. A parent in proxy mode can therefore change a child's curriculum by direct API call, including skipping topics or regenerating the curriculum.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) on curriculum mutation endpoints unless a route is explicitly designed as a parent-management action with assertParentAccess. Add proxy-mode break tests for each mutation.

title: Topic explanation lookup is not scoped to the requested subject/profile
description: GET /subjects/:subjectId/curriculum/topics/:topicId/explain passes the user-controlled topicId to explainTopicOrdering. The service first verifies the subject belongs to the caller, but then loads the topic by id alone, without tying it to that subject's latest curriculum or to subjects.profileId. A caller who knows another profile's topic UUID can combine it with one of their own subject IDs and get an LLM explanation containing the other topic's title and sort position.
recommendation: Load the topic through a join from curriculum_topics to curricula to subjects, requiring topicId, subjectId, and subjects.profileId to match. Return NotFound when the topic is not in the caller's subject curriculum.

title: Curriculum LLM endpoints are not metered or rate-limited
description: Several curriculum routes can trigger LLM calls: topic preview in POST /curriculum/topics, curriculum challenge/regeneration, and topic-order explanation. The metering middleware's LLM route patterns cover sessions, quiz, and dictation routes, but not these curriculum paths. An authenticated user can loop these endpoints against their own subject and burn provider capacity without quota decrement or a local rate limit.
recommendation: Add the LLM-consuming curriculum routes to metering or enforce a separate per-profile rate limit/idempotency policy around preview, challenge, and explanation calls.

title: Dictation review accepts an unbounded sentence payload before an LLM call
description: POST /dictation/review validates image size and applies a per-profile request-rate limit, but the imported dictationReviewInputSchema allows an unbounded sentences array and unbounded sentence strings. The handler then sends the sentence list to the vision LLM after validation. One authenticated request can therefore create a very large prompt/body and consume disproportionate memory, tokens, and provider cost despite the 10/minute rate limit.
recommendation: Add schema caps for sentence count and all sentence/chunk string lengths, enforce a total prompt character budget before calling reviewDictation, and reject oversized requests before the LLM call.

title: Dictation result can record practice activity against an unowned subject ID
description: POST /dictation/result accepts an optional subjectId from the client and passes it to recordDictationResult without verifying that the subject belongs to the resolved profile. The result row itself is profile-scoped, but the later practice-activity write stores the supplied subjectId as a foreign key, allowing cross-tenant subject references and corrupted practice/reporting data if an attacker supplies another profile's subject UUID.
recommendation: If subjectId is supplied, verify it with a profile-scoped subject lookup before recording the result or practice activity. Otherwise drop it or return 404/403 on mismatch.

title: Queued feedback loses the original message after email failure
description: When sendEmail returns unsent, the route emits app/feedback.delivery_failed with only profileId and category, then returns { success: true, queued: true }. The original feedback message, user id, app version, platform, OS, and support recipient are not persisted or included in the retry event, so the retry path cannot deliver the user's actual feedback. The Inngest consumer can only send a placeholder delivery-retry email, meaning transient Resend failures or missing RESEND_API_KEY silently lose feedback content while telling the client it was queued.
recommendation: Persist failed feedback submissions in a durable outbox table or include the full sanitized delivery payload in the retry event, then return queued only after the retry has enough data to deliver the original message.

title: Filing endpoints allow proxy-mode writes to child libraries
description: Both POST /filing/request-retry and POST /filing use requireProfileId() but never call assertNotProxyMode(). The /filing path resolves LLM output into DB records for the resolved profile, backfills session topicId, and marks suggestions picked/used. A parent/proxy session can therefore create or change a child profile's library state and queue retry work under the child profile, bypassing the repo's proxy-mode write guard used by comparable write routes.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) before queuing filing retries and before the main filing flow performs LLM calls or writes. If any parent-managed filing action is intentionally allowed, expose it as a separate endpoint with explicit server-side role checks and narrowed behavior.

title: Filing LLM calls bypass quota and retry controls
description: POST /filing directly calls fileToLibrary(), which invokes routeAndCall(), and POST /filing/request-retry queues app/filing.retry, whose worker runs the same LLM-backed filing flow. The metering middleware patterns cover session messages, quiz, dictation, and /sessions/:sessionId/retry-filing, but not /filing or /filing/request-retry. The legacy request-retry route also accepts any string sessionId and does not validate session ownership/state or claim the retry counter before queueing, unlike the canonical session retry route. An authenticated client can repeatedly burn LLM capacity and enqueue retry work outside quota/rate limits.
recommendation: Add quota/rate-limit enforcement for /filing and any remaining retry endpoint, validate retry payloads with filingRetryEventSchema, and remove or harden /filing/request-retry so it first loads the session for the verified profile and uses claimSessionForFilingRetry() with the existing retry cap.

title: OCR endpoint is authenticated but not metered or rate-limited
description: POST /ocr requires a profile and enforces a 5MB file cap, but then calls getOcrProvider().extractText(), which routes through the LLM provider. The centralized metering middleware does not include /ocr, so an authenticated user can repeatedly submit images and consume paid OCR/LLM capacity without quota decrement or route-specific throttling.
recommendation: Add /ocr to the centralized LLM metering/rate-limit policy, with per-profile quota accounting and refund behavior for provider failures.

title: Child learner-profile routes bypass child consent visibility checks
description: The parent-scoped child routes verify only the family link with assertParentAccess before reading, exporting, creating, or mutating the child learning profile. Global consentMiddleware evaluates c.get('profileMeta'), which is the scoped/active profile, not the child profile from the path parameter. In contrast, the dashboard child memory route explicitly calls assertChildDashboardDataVisible before returning child memory. A linked parent can therefore call routes such as GET /learner-profile/:profileId/export-text or GET /learner-profile/:profileId for a child whose consent is PENDING, PARENTAL_CONSENT_REQUESTED, or WITHDRAWN and receive or create raw learner memory despite the consent state.
recommendation: After assertParentAccess, enforce the child profile's consent status before all child data read/write routes. Keep any intentional consent-management exception narrow and explicit, and avoid getOrCreateLearningProfile for withdrawn or non-consented child profiles.

title: Tell Mentor endpoints invoke the LLM without quota or rate limiting
description: POST /learner-profile/tell and POST /learner-profile/:profileId/tell pass user-controlled text to parseLearnerInput, which calls routeAndCall. The global metering middleware's LLM route patterns include sessions, quiz generation, dictation, and retry filing, but not these learner-profile tell routes. An authenticated user can repeatedly call these endpoints with 500-character notes and burn LLM capacity without consuming quota or hitting an endpoint-specific rate limit.
recommendation: Add these routes to quota enforcement or introduce an equivalent service-level limiter for learner-memory LLM parsing. Include regression tests proving exhausted quota blocks both self and parent tell routes before routeAndCall executes.

title: Maintenance endpoints report queued even if the dispatch failed
description: The maintenance endpoints use safeSend for the Inngest dispatch and then always return { queued: true }. safeSend is designed for non-core side effects: it catches failures and timeouts, logs them, and returns void. Here the dispatch is the entire purpose of the endpoint, so an Inngest failure or timeout produces a 200 queued response even though no backfill may have been scheduled.
recommendation: Treat the Inngest send as core work for these endpoints: await inngest.send directly, return a 5xx on dispatch failure, or persist a durable maintenance request before acknowledging queued.

title: Proxy-mode parent can mark child nudges as read
description: The read-state mutation endpoints use only the resolved profileId from X-Profile-Id before calling markNudgeRead() and markAllNudgesRead(). profileScopeMiddleware verifies that the profile belongs to the authenticated account, but these handlers never call assertNotProxyMode(), even though that guard is the server-side protection against parent-on-child proxy writes. A parent in proxy mode can target a child profile and mark individual or all nudges addressed to that child as read, mutating child-owned notification state. The service-level toProfileId filter prevents cross-family IDOR, but it does not prevent this proxy-mode write bypass.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) before the markNudgeRead() and markAllNudgesRead() service calls, or split out an explicitly parent-authorized endpoint if parents are intended to manage this state. Add proxy-mode break tests covering profileMeta.isOwner=false with X-Proxy-Mode omitted and false.

title: Self-service onboarding writes bypass proxy-mode and parent-child access checks
description: The self-service PATCH endpoints update the active profile selected by X-Profile-Id and only rely on requireProfileId() plus the service accountId guard. The same file defines parent-on-behalf-of-child variants that first call assertParentAccess(), but a parent in proxy mode can instead set the active child profile and call the self-service /onboarding/language, /onboarding/pronouns, or /onboarding/interests/context routes. Because these handlers never call assertNotProxyMode(), they allow child profile personalization state, including pronouns and interests, to be changed through the proxy path rather than the explicit parent-authorized path.
recommendation: Add assertNotProxyMode(c) to the self-service onboarding write handlers so proxy sessions must use the explicit parent-on-behalf routes. Add tests proving non-owner/profileMeta.isOwner=false requests are rejected even if X-Proxy-Mode is omitted or set to false.

title: Proxy-mode child profile can add parking-lot items
description: The POST /sessions/:sessionId/parking-lot handler writes a new parking-lot item after resolving profileId, but it never calls assertNotProxyMode(). A parent/guardian proxy session can send X-Profile-Id for a child profile, pass the session ownership check for that child profile, and add learner-facing questions to the child's session data despite proxy mode being intended as read-only for writes.
recommendation: Import and call assertNotProxyMode(c) before reading the request body or calling getSession/addParkingLotItem.

title: Topic progress leaks topic metadata for arbitrary topic IDs
description: The route accepts a user-controlled subjectId and topicId, verifies only that subjectId belongs to the active profile, then delegates to getTopicProgress. In apps/api/src/services/progress.ts, the service checks the subject via the scoped repository but fetches curriculumTopics by topicId with an unscoped global lookup and returns topic.title and topic.description. An authenticated user who knows or obtains another profile's topic UUID can call this endpoint using any owned subjectId and receive that topic's title/description even though the topic is not in their curriculum.
recommendation: Resolve the topic through curriculumTopics -> curricula -> subjects with subjects.profileId = profileId and curricula.subjectId = subjectId, or use the scoped curriculum topic helper and verify the subject chain before returning metadata. Return 404 for unknown or unowned topics and add a negative-path test with an owned subject plus foreign topicId.

title: Quiz completion can be manipulated to inflate score, XP, and mastery
description: The check endpoint reveals correctAnswer on a wrong submission without recording that attempt, and the complete endpoint later accepts client-supplied results for scoring. The service recomputes correctness from answerGiven, but it does not enforce one result per question or derive completion from server-recorded checked attempts. A client can call /quiz/rounds/:id/check with a wrong answer to learn the correct answer, then call /quiz/rounds/:id/complete with correct answers, duplicate questionIndex entries, or both. Those entries are counted when calculating score, XP, practice events, and mastery updates, corrupting learner progress and parent-facing metrics.
recommendation: Persist per-question attempts when checking answers and have completion consume server-side attempt state, or delay correct-answer disclosure until the answer is locked. Also enforce unique questionIndex values, cap results to the round total, and reject duplicate or missing required results.

title: Resend webhook is mounted behind Clerk auth
description: The route is documented as not being behind Clerk auth and performs Svix signature verification itself, but it is mounted under /v1 after the global auth middleware and /v1/webhooks/resend is not in auth.ts PUBLIC_PATHS. A real Resend delivery has no Clerk bearer token, so the request is rejected before this handler can verify svix-id/svix-signature. That makes bounce/complaint/delivery webhook processing effectively unreachable in the full app; the route-only tests do not catch the middleware-chain behavior.
recommendation: Add /v1/webhooks/resend to the auth middleware public path list and add a full app-level regression test that posts to /v1/webhooks/resend without a Clerk token but with a valid Svix signature.

title: Teaching-preference writes bypass parent proxy-mode authorization
description: PUT and DELETE /subjects/:subjectId/teaching-preference are profile-scoped writes, but unlike recall-test and relearn they do not call assertNotProxyMode(c). A parent acting on a child profile can send the child X-Profile-Id; profileScopeMiddleware will verify the child belongs to the account and set profileId, then these handlers write or delete teachingPreferences for that child. This bypasses the server-derived proxy-mode write guard intended to make parent-child proxy sessions read-only.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) as the first line in both PUT and DELETE handlers, and add break tests for profileMeta.isOwner=false and X-Proxy-Mode:true.

title: Evaluate eligibility leaks topic titles across profiles
description: GET /topics/:topicId/evaluate-eligibility passes the user-controlled topicId to checkEvaluateEligibility. In apps/api/src/services/evaluate-data.ts, the retention card lookup is scoped, but the topic title is fetched from curriculumTopics by topicId without verifying the curriculum belongs to the active profile; when no scoped card exists, the response still includes topicTitle. Because curriculum topics are profile-owned through subjects, a known foreign topic UUID can disclose another learner's topic title.
recommendation: Before returning topic metadata, verify topicId through curriculumTopics -> curricula -> subjects with subjects.profileId = profileId. Return 404 or a generic unowned-topic response without the title when the chain is not owned.

title: Recall tests can create retention state for unowned topics
description: POST /retention/recall-test accepts a topicId and calls processRecallTest. In apps/api/src/services/retention-data.ts, processRecallTest looks up a scoped retention card and, if none exists, calls ensureRetentionCard(db, profileId, input.topicId) before verifying that the topic belongs to the profile. It then fetches the topic globally for LLM context and response data. A user with a known foreign topic UUID can create profile-local retention state pointing at another profile's curriculum topic and may receive the topic title/description via recall hint or remediation responses.
recommendation: Verify topic ownership through the curriculum/subject/profile chain before ensureRetentionCard, LLM grading, or response construction. Reject unowned topics with NotFoundError and add a negative-path test.

title: Recall-test LLM calls are not metered
description: Standard recall submissions call processRecallTest, which invokes evaluateRecallQuality and routeAndCall for LLM grading. The global metering middleware only matches session message/stream, recall-bridge, evaluate-depth, quiz, dictation, and retry-filing paths; /retention/recall-test is not included. An authenticated client can consume LLM capacity through recall grading without decrementing quota, bounded only by per-topic cooldown.
recommendation: Add /retention/recall-test to the metered LLM route patterns or introduce an equivalent per-profile quota/rate limit before evaluateRecallQuality runs. Ensure failed validation and cooldown responses do not consume quota unnecessarily.

title: RevenueCat idempotency/order check is not atomic with subscription updates
description: The route checks isRevenuecatEventProcessed before dispatch, then handlers update subscription state later. The imported update helpers read the current subscription and perform an unconditional update by subscription id, so two concurrent RevenueCat events for the same account can both pass the pre-check against the old lastRevenuecatEventTimestampMs. Whichever update commits last wins, allowing an older EXPIRATION/CANCELLATION retry to overwrite a newer RENEWAL/INITIAL_PURCHASE despite the timestamp ordering logic.
recommendation: Enforce event ordering in the write itself: use a transaction/advisory lock per account or a conditional UPDATE that only applies when lastRevenuecatEventTimestampMs is null or <= the incoming timestamp, and return a skipped result when the conditional update affects zero rows. Cover concurrent old/new webhook delivery in tests.

title: Production sandbox rejection still mutates billing state
description: The production SANDBOX guard runs after account resolution, the idempotency check, and ensureFreeSubscription. ensureFreeSubscription can insert an active free subscription for a production account before the sandbox event is rejected, contradicting the comment that sandbox events must not mutate production state. For an account whose trial subscription creation previously failed, this can also lock in a free subscription and prevent normal trial provisioning.
recommendation: Move the SANDBOX-in-production rejection immediately after payload validation and before any account lookup, idempotency check, subscription provisioning, or KV/database mutation. Add a test asserting ensureFreeSubscription is not called for production SANDBOX events.

title: Non-LLM session mutation endpoints bypass the server-derived proxy-mode write guard
description: Several state-changing session endpoints require a profileId but never call assertNotProxyMode. Metering protects the LLM endpoints such as messages/stream/retry-filing/evaluate-depth/recall-bridge, but these unmetered mutation paths can still be called with X-Profile-Id set to a child profile. The services scope writes to that profileId, but they do not reject profileMeta.isOwner === false, so a parent in proxy mode can close a child session, add system_prompt/session events, change input mode, sync homework state, flag content, or skip/submit summaries and trigger completion side effects. This violates the repo's read-only proxy-mode boundary.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) before every session write that is not explicitly parent-management behavior, or add a route-level write middleware for these POST/PATCH paths. Add break tests where profileMeta.isOwner is false and X-Proxy-Mode is omitted.

title: Client-controlled system prompts are persisted and replayed as LLM system-role messages
description: POST /sessions/:sessionId/system-prompt accepts arbitrary content from the client and records it as a system_prompt event. The session service later maps system_prompt events to role: system in exchange history before calling the LLM. A direct API caller can therefore inject arbitrary system-role instructions into the next model turn, rather than having that text treated as user data. The impact is scoped to the caller's accessible session, but it can alter tutor behavior and envelope-driven state signals with higher authority than a normal user message.
recommendation: Do not accept arbitrary system-role content from clients. Replace this endpoint with a small enum of allowed client actions that the server maps to fixed prompts, or store the client text as data/metadata and include it in the LLM prompt as a sanitized user/server_note block rather than role: system.

title: Active-profile settings writes do not reject parent proxy sessions
description: The active-profile settings write endpoints update notification preferences, learning mode, push token, celebration level, analogy domain, and native language after requireProfileId(), but they do not call assertNotProxyMode. profileScopeMiddleware verifies the profile belongs to the account and sets profileMeta.isOwner=false for child profiles; these handlers never consume that signal. A parent acting in child proxy mode can directly mutate child learning/settings state even though proxy mode is intended to be read-only except for explicit parent-management routes.
recommendation: Apply assertNotProxyMode to active-profile settings writes. Keep explicit parent-management behavior on separate endpoints that take childProfileId and enforce assertParentAccess, so proxy-mode reads cannot silently become child-profile writes.

title: Progress write paths omit the parent-proxy write guard
description: The route correctly uses requireProfileId, so this is not cross-account IDOR, but mutating progress operations do not call assertNotProxyMode. GET /progress/milestones calls listRecentMilestones, which can backfill milestone rows, and POST /progress/refresh calls refreshProgressSnapshot, which upserts progress snapshots, stores milestones, and queues celebrations. A parent/proxy session can therefore mutate a child profile's progress-derived state by selecting the child X-Profile-Id even though non-owner profile writes are supposed to be blocked by the server-derived proxy guard.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) before any progress endpoint that can write, or move milestone backfill/refresh work to an owner-only/background path. Add proxy-mode regression tests with profileMeta.isOwner=false and no X-Proxy-Mode header.

title: Paid entitlements are granted from unverified Stripe metadata
description: After Stripe signature verification, the checkout handler reads accountId and tier from session.metadata and immediately calls activateSubscriptionFromCheckout with that tier. The route does not check session.mode, session.status, session.payment_status, the paid line item price, or a freshly retrieved Stripe subscription status. Subscription update handling also trusts stripeSubscription.metadata.tier for quota changes instead of deriving the tier from configured Stripe price IDs. If asynchronous payment methods are enabled, a user can complete checkout while payment is still pending/unpaid and receive paid LLM quota before a later failure event. If the Stripe customer portal allows plan changes, a downgrade can change the subscription item price while stale metadata continues to advertise the previous higher tier, preserving higher quota incorrectly.
recommendation: Treat Stripe metadata only as correlation data. Before activating or changing quota, verify the Checkout Session is subscription mode and paid/eligible, retrieve or expand the subscription items, map the Stripe price ID to the configured internal tier, and handle async payment success/failure events explicitly.

title: Stripe event ordering loses distinct events created in the same second
description: The route converts Stripe event.created, which has only second-level precision, into lastStripeEventTimestamp for all subscription state changes. The imported updateSubscriptionFromWebhook helper skips incoming updates when the incoming timestamp is less than or equal to the stored timestamp. Distinct Stripe events for the same subscription can legitimately share the same created second, so whichever one is processed second is treated as already processed. For example, a payment_failed and payment_succeeded, or subscription.updated and subscription.deleted, emitted in the same second can leave the account active, past_due, or on the wrong quota indefinitely.
recommendation: Store and pass the Stripe event ID alongside event.created. Deduplicate exact event IDs, reject only strictly older timestamps, and allow distinct events with equal timestamps to be processed using event-type-specific ordering or a webhook event ledger.

title: Subject write endpoints bypass the proxy-mode write guard
description: Subject creation, language setup, curriculum retry dispatch, and subject update all resolve profileId but never call assertNotProxyMode(). Since profileScopeMiddleware permits an authenticated account to select a child profile and records profileMeta.isOwner=false, a parent/guardian proxy session can create or edit the child's subjects, change language setup, or dispatch curriculum generation jobs for the child.
recommendation: Call assertNotProxyMode(c) at the start of every subject write/dispatch handler. Keep read-only list/get/classify behavior separate from write behavior.

title: Subject LLM flows are outside centralized quota enforcement
description: The resolve, classify, create, and retry-curriculum flows can invoke LLM-backed services, but meteringMiddleware only covers session, quiz, dictation, recall-bridge, evaluate-depth, and retry-filing patterns. An authenticated user can repeatedly call subject resolution/classification or create/retry curriculum work without decrementing LLM quota; retry-curriculum can also enqueue duplicate background generation while books remain stuck.
recommendation: Add these LLM-backed subject routes and retry dispatches to centralized quota/rate limiting. Add idempotency/deduplication for retry-curriculum events keyed by subject/book/profile.

title: Outbox spillover has no server-side rate limit
description: POST /support/outbox-spillover accepts up to 50 entries per request, each with up to 8000 characters, and writes them to support_messages. The only duplicate control is uniqueness on profileId plus clientId in recordOutboxSpillover, but clientId is attacker-controlled, so an authenticated client can continuously submit unique IDs and fill support storage or spam human review queues.
recommendation: Add a per-profile rolling rate limit before insertion, ideally also enforcing a byte or entry budget. Return a typed 429 response and add boundary tests for repeated unique clientIds.

title: Test seed/reset can modify or delete non-seed accounts outside production
description: The route is blocked in production and requires TEST_SEED_SECRET outside development, so this is not a production auth bypass. However, in staging/dev the /__test/seed endpoint accepts an arbitrary email and calls seedScenario, whose service deletes existing accounts for that email when clerkUserId starts with user_. Real Clerk user IDs also start with user_, so a seed request for a real staging user's email can delete that account's local data. The Clerk helper also reuses an existing Clerk user by email and PATCHes its password, external_id, and bypass_client_trust, returning the seed password to the caller. Separately, /__test/reset passes an arbitrary prefix to resetDatabase, whose prefix branch deletes accounts by email prefix without requiring a seed marker. A mistaken or malicious test-secret holder can therefore corrupt or delete non-seed data in non-production environments.
recommendation: Only mutate accounts with a durable seed marker stored locally or verified from Clerk external_id. Do not treat all user_* Clerk IDs as seed-created. For prefix cleanup, require a seed marker plus a constrained test prefix, and avoid resetting passwords on pre-existing Clerk users unless they are already marked as seed users.

title: Vocabulary mutations are allowed from parent-proxy sessions
description: The create, review, and delete handlers unwrap a server-verified profileId and then call mutating vocabulary services without assertNotProxyMode. The service layer scopes writes to that profileId, which prevents cross-account access, but it does not enforce the owner-vs-child proxy boundary. An authenticated parent viewing a child profile can create vocabulary, mark review quality, or delete vocabulary for the child by sending the child X-Profile-Id while omitting or falsifying X-Proxy-Mode; the existing proxy guard would reject this if it were called.
recommendation: Import and call assertNotProxyMode(c) in every vocabulary write handler before invoking createVocabulary, reviewVocabulary, or deleteVocabulary. Add tests proving profileMeta.isOwner=false is rejected even when X-Proxy-Mode is absent or false.

title: Email-only Clerk subject reclaim can transfer an existing account
description: findOrCreateAccount() treats any existing local account with the same email as reclaimable when the Clerk sub is new, then rewrites accounts.clerkUserId to the new sub. There is no proof that this is the same human, no recovery token, no check that the old account was intentionally deleted, and no verified-email claim check in this service boundary. If an attacker can obtain a Clerk session whose email claim matches a victim or recycled email address, their Clerk user becomes bound to the victim's local account, including profiles, learning history, and billing state.
recommendation: Do not automatically reclaim by email alone. Require an explicit account recovery/migration flow with a signed one-time token or a trusted Clerk webhook/state transition, verify the email is primary and verified, and constrain the update to accounts in an expected reclaimable/deleted state.

title: Assessment creation accepts unowned subject and topic IDs
description: createAssessment() inserts the authenticated profileId together with caller-supplied subjectId and topicId without verifying that the subject belongs to that profile or that the topic belongs to that subject through the curricula chain. The database foreign keys only prove those global IDs exist. An authenticated user who knows another profile's subject/topic UUID can create assessment, retention, and activity rows in their own profile that reference another tenant's curriculum objects, corrupting progress and creating cross-tenant references.
