A parent acting on a child profile, or a modified client, can call the same subject creation endpoint with the child's verified X-Profile-Id and create curriculum data for that child despite proxy mode being intended read-only.
recommendation: Add assertNotProxyMode(c) to POST /subjects and other subject write routes such as PATCH /subjects/:id, then add negative-path tests proving non-owner profile requests are rejected even when X-Proxy-Mode is omitted.

title: Homework camera flow invokes unmetered LLM-backed OCR and subject classification
description: The camera flow calls subject classification, OCR processing, and subject creation from user-controlled homework text/images. Tracing these calls shows /v1/ocr uses the Gemini OCR provider, /v1/subjects/classify calls routeAndCall, and subject creation can trigger LLM-backed subject/language/curriculum detection. The API metering middleware covers session, quiz, and dictation LLM routes, but not /ocr, /subjects/classify, or /subjects. An authenticated attacker can repeatedly exercise these paths to consume paid LLM/OCR capacity outside the quota system.
recommendation: Enforce server-side quota or a dedicated rate limit for /ocr, /subjects/classify, and LLM-backed subject creation. Client-side debouncing can improve UX, but the abuse protection must live on the API routes.

title: Parent proxy sessions can mutate child subjects from Library
description: Library is available in the learner tab shape used during parent proxy mode, but the screen does not check useParentProxy before exposing add/manage subject controls. The manage modal calls useUpdateSubject to pause, archive, resume, or restore subjects. The traced API route PATCH /subjects/:id only requires requireProfileId and calls updateSubject scoped to that profile; it does not call assertNotProxyMode. Because profileScopeMiddleware accepts a child X-Profile-Id that belongs to the parent account, a parent in proxy mode can mutate the child curriculum despite the read-only proxy-mode rule.
recommendation: Hide or disable Library write controls in proxy mode, and enforce the boundary on the API by adding assertNotProxyMode to subject write routes such as POST /subjects, PATCH /subjects/:id, PUT /subjects/:id/language-setup, and retry-curriculum. Add break tests with non-owner profileMeta and no X-Proxy-Mode header.

title: Parent proxy can bypass client-only memory write guard and impersonate learner memory updates
description: The screen hides itself in parent proxy mode with only a client-side Redirect, but the self-service memory actions still map to API routes that trust the resolved X-Profile-Id and do not call the server-side assertNotProxyMode guard. In the traced API route, POST /learner-profile/tell records the note with source 'learner', while the parent-on-behalf route records source 'parent'. A parent with a valid session can set X-Profile-Id to the child profile and call the self endpoint directly, bypassing the UI redirect and writing/deleting/toggling child memory as if the learner did it.
recommendation: Add assertNotProxyMode(c) to all self-service learner-profile mutation routes and the self onboarding mutation routes used here. Keep the explicit parent-on-behalf routes for legitimate parent actions, and add break tests for non-owner profileMeta with X-Proxy-Mode omitted or false.

title: Tell Mentor invokes an unmetered LLM endpoint
description: Submitting Tell Mentor calls useTellMentor, which reaches POST /learner-profile/tell. That route calls parseLearnerInput(), which calls routeAndCall() for LLM parsing. The metering middleware's LLM route allowlist does not include /learner-profile/tell or /learner-profile/:profileId/tell, and there is no route-specific rate limit. An authenticated user can repeatedly submit up to 500-character notes and burn LLM capacity outside quota enforcement.
recommendation: Add the Tell Mentor routes to the server metering/rate-limit policy, or install a dedicated per-profile/account limiter with clear 429 responses. Add tests proving quota/proxy checks run before parseLearnerInput.

title: Notification toggles can overwrite preferences after a failed settings load
description: The screen only checks `isLoading` from `useNotificationSettings()` and never handles the error/no-data state. If the settings query fails, `notifPrefs` is undefined, but all toggles remain enabled and each handler builds a full PUT body from fallback defaults. A user toggling one preference after a transient load failure can overwrite unrelated notification settings with defaults such as `reviewReminders: false`, `dailyReminders: false`, and email digests set to true.
recommendation: Track `isError`/missing data and render a retry/error state or disable toggles until preferences are loaded. Prefer partial update endpoints or construct full update bodies only from confirmed server data.

title: Language setup write bypasses parent-proxy read-only guard
description: LanguageSetup sends the route-provided subjectId into configureLanguageSubject before starting the first session. The traced API route PUT /subjects/:id/language-setup requires a profileId but does not call assertNotProxyMode, while the service writes native-language preferences and regenerates the subject curriculum. A parent operating in a child proxy profile can therefore deep-link to this screen, or call the same RPC directly with the child X-Profile-Id, and mutate the child's language-learning setup even though comparable learner-write routes block proxy mode server-side.
recommendation: Add assertNotProxyMode(c) to the language-setup API route before configureLanguageSubject, and add a negative-path test where a non-owner profile is resolved through X-Profile-Id with and without X-Proxy-Mode. If parent-managed language setup is intended, expose a separate parent-scoped endpoint with assertParentAccess instead of allowing the self-service route in proxy mode.

title: Pronouns age gate is enforced only in the client
description: The screen hides itself for profiles below PRONOUNS_PROMPT_MIN_AGE using activeProfile.birthYear, but the save and skip paths call useUpdatePronouns, which maps to PATCH /onboarding/pronouns. The traced API route and updatePronouns service validate only the payload shape and profile/account ownership; they do not enforce the minimum age server-side. A rooted client or direct API caller authenticated as an under-13 learner can store pronouns despite the product rule that learners below the cutoff skip this field and leave it declined/null.
recommendation: Move the self-service age gate to the API using server-derived profile metadata or a DB lookup. Reject /onboarding/pronouns for profiles below PRONOUNS_PROMPT_MIN_AGE unless the request uses an explicit parent-managed endpoint guarded by assertParentAccess, and cover this with a negative-path test.

title: Parent proxy mode can archive a child subject
description: The screen exposes a destructive subject archive action without checking parent-proxy mode: `hideSubject` calls `useUpdateSubject().mutateAsync({ subjectId, status: 'archived' })`, and the button is always rendered for any loaded subject. The corresponding API path `PATCH /subjects/:id` only requires a resolved profile id and calls `updateSubject`; it does not call `assertNotProxyMode`, while other write routes do. Because parent proxy requests resolve the child profile through `X-Profile-Id`, an authenticated parent acting in read-only proxy mode can still archive the child's subject, bypassing the intended server-side proxy write guard.
recommendation: Add `assertNotProxyMode(c)` to `PATCH /subjects/:id` and any other subject write routes that should be forbidden in proxy mode. Also hide or disable the `progress-subject-hide` action when `useParentProxy().isParentProxy` is true, and add a negative-path route test using a non-owner `profileMeta`.

title: Round-detail cache is not scoped by active profile
description: The screen renders data from useRoundDetail(roundId). The imported hook uses React Query key ['quiz-round-detail', roundId] with staleTime 60s and does not include activeProfile.id, while profile switching does not reset that query key. A completed round fetched under profile A can therefore be served from client cache after switching to profile B when the same roundId route is opened from history, a deep link, or stale navigation. The server would reject profile B for profile A's round, but the fresh cache can render profile A's theme, questions, correct answers, and submitted answers without a new authorized request.
recommendation: Include activeProfile.id in the useRoundDetail query key and add quiz detail keys to the profile-switch reset list. Add a regression test that switches profiles after viewing a completed round and verifies the old detail is not rendered.

title: Active round IDs crash the history detail screen
description: The screen casts every round detail response to CompletedRoundDetail, then destructures results and calls results.find during render. The API returns an active-round shape without results for active rounds, so opening /quiz/[roundId] with an active round ID can throw instead of showing an error state or redirecting to play.
recommendation: Check that the fetched round is completed and has a results array before rendering this view. Redirect active rounds to the play flow or show an actionable error fallback.

title: Duplicate question results can inflate quiz score and XP
description: QuizPlayScreen submits the client-held results array to the completion mutation. Following that import shows the server revalidates correctness but does not enforce unique questionIndex values or cap validated results to the round total: every in-bounds result is scored. An authenticated client can bypass the UI and submit repeated answers for the same questionIndex in a single completion request, producing score > total, inflated XP, and corrupted practice/progress events before the round is marked completed.
recommendation: Enforce one result per question on the server, cap validation to questions.length, compute score over unique question indices, and reject duplicate questionIndex submissions. Add a negative test that duplicate correct results cannot produce score or XP above the round maximum.

title: Proxy-mode session write protection relies on a client-side redirect for non-metered writes
description: SessionLayout redirects parent-proxy users away from the session UI, but that is only a client-side navigation guard. Tracing the server routes used by the session flow shows assertNotProxyMode is applied to session creation, clear-continuation-depth, interleaved sessions, and metered LLM routes, but non-metered session writes such as close, system-prompt, events, input-mode, homework-state, flag, summary submit/skip, and parking-lot writes do not consistently call the server-side proxy guard. profileScope verifies that the child profile belongs to the authenticated account, but it does not reject non-owner writes unless assertNotProxyMode is invoked. A parent can bypass this Redirect by calling those API endpoints directly with the child X-Profile-Id and mutate child session state.
recommendation: Enforce proxy-mode authorization on the API boundary for every session and session-adjacent write route, preferably with route-group middleware or explicit assertNotProxyMode(c) calls. Add negative tests with profileMeta.isOwner=false and with X-Proxy-Mode omitted/false for close, input-mode, homework-state, summary, flag, system-prompt/events, and parking-lot writes.

title: Deep-link-controlled imageUri can be read and uploaded as homework image data
description: SessionScreen accepts imageUri directly from route params, only normalizes duplicate params with firstParam(), and passes it into useImageBase64. That hook calls FileSystem.readAsStringAsync(uri) without restricting scheme, directory, or provenance, then useSessionStreaming attaches the resulting base64 to the first streamed homework message. The screen also auto-sends problemText when no routeSessionId is present. Because the app exposes the mentomate URL scheme, a malicious deep link opened by a signed-in user can provide mode=homework, problemText, and imageUri=file:///... or another readable URI, causing the app to read an accessible local file/content URI and send it to the API/LLM if a subject is provided or resolved.
recommendation: Do not trust imageUri from navigation params. Pass an app-generated capture handle instead, or validate the URI against a dedicated homework-capture directory under FileSystem.cacheDirectory/documentDirectory plus expected MIME/extension. Avoid auto-attaching route-supplied files unless they came from the camera/gallery flow and the user confirmed them in-app.

title: Session subject-resolution flow reaches LLM endpoints that are outside quota metering
description: The session screen wires useClassifySubject and useResolveSubject into the first-message subject selection path. Tracing those hooks shows /subjects/classify and /subjects/resolve call LLM-backed services via routeAndCall, but meteringMiddleware only covers session message/stream, recall/evaluate-depth, retry-filing, quiz, and dictation routes. An authenticated client can repeatedly drive subject resolution/classification directly, or through first-turn session classification when no subject is set, without decrementing quota or hitting a dedicated rate limit.
recommendation: Add the subject LLM routes to the same quota/rate-limit protection as other LLM-consuming endpoints, or add a dedicated per-account/profile rate limiter for subject classification, subject resolution, and any subject-creation path that calls LLMs.

title: Top-up purchase can be submitted more than once before the UI lock applies
description: The top-up button relies on React state (`topUpPurchasing` / `topUpPolling`) to disable repeat presses, but `handleTopUp` has no synchronous in-flight ref. A rapid double tap can enter `handleTopUp` twice before `setTopUpPurchasing(true)` is reflected in the rendered `disabled` prop, causing concurrent `purchase.mutateAsync(topUpPkg)` calls for a consumable IAP. The later "Check later" action also clears `topUpPolling` while the original confirmation loop is still running, re-enabling the buy button before confirmation finishes. In a billing flow this can lead to duplicate purchase sheets or duplicate charges.
recommendation: Add a synchronous `useRef` lock for top-up purchase/confirmation and check it at the start of `handleTopUp`. Keep that lock held until the purchase flow is truly finished, or introduce an explicit cancellation token if "Check later" is meant to abandon only the UI spinner.

title: Child paywall is unreachable behind ParentOnly
description: `SubscriptionScreen` wraps all of `SubscriptionContent` in `ParentOnly`, which renders nothing and redirects for `child` and `impersonated-child` roles. The child paywall branch is inside `SubscriptionContent`, so the `isChild && (trialOrExpired || quotaExhausted)` path cannot render in production. Children with expired/cancelled subscriptions or exhausted quota are bounced away instead of seeing the paywall, notify-parent action, and safe navigation links.
recommendation: Move the child/proxy decision outside `ParentOnly`, or have `SubscriptionScreen` render `ChildPaywall` for child quota/expiry states before applying the parent-only wrapper to owner billing controls. Add a test that mocks `useActiveProfileRole()` as `child`.

title: Client-supplied subjectId can bypass topic ownership resolution
description: TopicDetailScreen only resolves the topic's subject when subjectId is absent. If a deep link supplies any subjectId owned by the active profile plus a topicId from another profile, the screen calls useTopicProgress with those values. The server-side getTopicProgress path verifies that the subject belongs to the profile, but then fetches curriculumTopics by topicId globally and returns the topic title/description without proving that the topic belongs to that subject/profile. This creates an IDOR-style metadata disclosure for known topic UUIDs.
recommendation: Fix the server service to resolve topics through curriculumTopics -> curricula -> subjects and require both curricula.subjectId === subjectId and subjects.profileId === profileId before returning any topic data. Prefer always using a server-resolved subject for topic detail deep links, and add a negative-path test with an owned subjectId and foreign topicId.

title: Recall-test LLM calls are not covered by quota metering
description: handleSend posts arbitrary recall answers to /retention/recall-test through useSubmitRecallTest. That server route calls evaluateRecallQuality, which invokes the LLM router, but the API metering middleware's LLM route patterns do not include /retention/recall-test. The per-topic cooldown is not a quota control and can be multiplied across many topic IDs, so authenticated clients can burn LLM capacity without the normal metering path.
recommendation: Add POST /retention/recall-test to the metering middleware's LLM-consuming route list, enforce quota before evaluateRecallQuality runs, and add a regression test proving recall-test decrements quota or rejects exhausted accounts.

title: Recall test accepts topicId without ownership verification
description: RecallTestScreen trusts the route topicId and sends it directly in both standard and dont_remember submissions. The server's processRecallTest auto-creates or loads a retention card for profileId + topicId before proving that the topic belongs to the active profile, then looks up curriculumTopics globally to build LLM context, hints, and remediation metadata. With a known foreign topic UUID, an authenticated user can create profile-local retention state linked to that topic and disclose its title or description through recall-test responses.
recommendation: Before ensureRetentionCard, LLM evaluation, or hint/remediation construction, verify topic ownership through curriculumTopics -> curricula -> subjects.profileId. Return 404 for unowned topic IDs and add a break test using a topic owned by a different profile.

title: Repeated dont_remember taps can overcount recall failures
description: handleDontRemember has no pending or streaming guard, and the input accessory remains tappable while an animated response is streaming. Each tap posts another dont_remember attempt; the server path intentionally skips the standard cooldown write for dont_remember, so rapid taps can increment failure state multiple times and prematurely push the topic into remediation.
recommendation: Disable the dont_remember control while submitRecallTest is pending or while isStreaming is true, and consider server-side idempotency or a short cooldown for dont_remember attempts.

title: Cancel does not cancel the in-flight relearn start
description: The screen starts the relearn mutation and always navigates to the new session from the mutation onSuccess callback. The visible Cancel button only sets isSubmitting=false and clears the local error state; it does not abort the request or mark the result stale. If the user taps Cancel while the POST is in flight, the request can still create a session and router.push to it after cancellation.
recommendation: Track a request generation/cancelled flag or use an abortable mutation path so stale onSuccess callbacks are ignored after Cancel. Alternatively remove the Cancel affordance if the operation cannot be cancelled.

title: Pending auth redirect can be overwritten with home after a remount
description: The screen tries to preserve a previously remembered auth redirect by passing `peekPendingAuthRedirect() ?? '/(app)/home'` as the fallback to `toInternalAppRedirectPath()`, then later persists `requestedRedirectRef.current` during session activation. The imported helper currently ignores that fallback for undefined values and returns `/(app)/home`, so if the sign-in screen remounts after an OAuth/browser handoff without a `redirectTo` param, `activateSession()` can overwrite the remembered deep-link target with home. This is not an open redirect, but it can break protected deep-link restoration.
recommendation: Honor the fallback inside `toInternalAppRedirectPath()` or initialize `requestedRedirectRef` directly from `peekPendingAuthRedirect()` when no local/browser redirect is present; add a regression test for an OAuth remount with only a pending redirect.

title: Raw React Query keys are sent to Sentry
description: The global QueryCache onError handler captures exceptions with queryKey: JSON.stringify(query.queryKey). Traced query keys include user-controlled learner search text and stable identifiers such as profile, session, report, and date parameters. Whenever one of those queries errors, sensitive learning activity or pseudonymous account data can be sent to third-party telemetry as tags. Sentry profile gating reduces some exposure for minors, but it does not sanitize adult or consented-profile data and is not a sufficient mitigation.
recommendation: Do not send raw query keys to Sentry. Log only a redacted query namespace, or hash/drop dynamic values such as search text, profile IDs, session IDs, and report IDs before capture.

title: Consent request can target arbitrary same-account profiles
description: The screen trusts the URL-provided profileId and sends it as childProfileId when creating or resending a consent request. The traced API path only verifies that childProfileId belongs to the authenticated account, not that the active profile is allowed to initiate consent for that target, that the target is a non-owner child, or that the target currently requires consent. Because the emailed token can later be denied through the unauthenticated consent response path, and that path deletes the profile tied to the token, a same-account non-owner profile could create a consent token for an owner/adult/sibling profile and cause destructive cross-profile data loss.
recommendation: Do not trust the route profileId as the authority. Server-side, require the target to be the active pending-consent profile or require a verified owner/parent relationship, verify the profile actually requires consent and is not the owner, and reject consent denial deletes for invalid/adult/owner targets. Client-side, reject profileId values that do not match the expected active profile.

title: Direct profile creation can bypass profile and consent gates
description: The root create-profile route is reachable directly and relies on client-side state such as params.for and activeProfile?.isOwner to decide whether it is adding a child. It then posts to /profiles and may switch to the returned profile. Tracing the API shows /v1/profiles is exempt from consent middleware, and additional profile creation/auto-consent is authorized at the account level rather than by verifying the current active profile is an adult, consented owner. On a multi-profile-capable account, a restricted or child profile that can deep-link to this screen could create or switch into another profile and bypass the normal app consent/profile gate.
recommendation: Move this authorization to the server. Require a verified adult/consented owner profile for non-first profile creation, child-profile consent grants, and profile switching out of consent-gated states. Treat the route's UI checks as hints only.

title: Minimum-age enforcement uses birth year instead of full birth date
description: The UI collects a full birth date but sends only birthYear to the API. The server-side consent check uses currentYear - birthYear, so a child who is still 10 but turns 11 later in the calendar year is treated as 11 and can avoid the parental-consent flow.
recommendation: Send and validate the full birth date on the server, and enforce the exact minimum age using month and day. Client-side validation can mirror this, but the API must be authoritative.

title: Profile creation timeout can leave an in-flight create request active
description: The 30 second timeout sets loading to false and shows an error, but it does not abort the pending /profiles POST. A retry can submit another create request while the first is still in flight, which can create duplicate child/profile records or consume profile slots unexpectedly.
recommendation: Abort the request on timeout or track the active submission so stale completions are ignored and retries are blocked until the prior request is definitively resolved or cancelled.

title: Subject creation flow can burn LLM calls outside quota enforcement
description: The screen sends arbitrary user input to the subject resolver and subject creation endpoints via resolveSubject.mutateAsync() and createSubject.mutateAsync(). Tracing those API routes shows /subjects/resolve calls an LLM through resolveSubjectName(), and subject creation can also invoke LLM-backed language/type/structure generation. The API metering middleware does not include these subject routes, so any authenticated user can repeatedly drive paid LLM calls outside the quota/rate-limit paths. This is mitigated by Clerk auth but still exploitable by a low-privilege account.
recommendation: Add server-side quota/rate limiting for /subjects/resolve and all subject creation paths that can invoke LLMs. Prefer enforcing it in the API metering layer, add per-profile/account limits, and cache or debounce identical resolver inputs where appropriate.

title: Resolve timeout does not cancel late subject results
description: The 30 second resolving timeout only flips UI state back to idle/error. It does not abort the in-flight resolveSubject request or mark that specific request as stale. A late response can still call setSuggestion(), transition state, or call doCreate() after the user has seen a timeout and potentially retried, causing confusing stale UI updates or duplicate subject creation attempts.
recommendation: Track a request sequence id or use AbortController/cancellation support so timed-out or superseded resolver responses are ignored before they can update state or create a subject.

title: Non-owner profiles can switch into owner context
description: Profile switching only prompts when the active profile is the owner and the target is a child. In every other case, including a non-owner child tapping the owner row, the screen immediately calls switchProfile(profile.id). The traced server endpoint only checks same-account ownership, so after the switch the API client sends the owner profile id and server-derived profileMeta.isOwner becomes true. That bypasses profile/parent-proxy boundaries and exposes owner-only account actions to a non-owner profile. The Add profile action is also shown without an owner-role check.
recommendation: Enforce profile-switch authorization server-side using the current active profile and role, not just account ownership. Non-owner profiles should only switch to themselves unless a parent reauth/PIN or equivalent owner proof succeeds. Hide or disable owner rows and add-profile actions for non-owner profiles, and keep this route behind the same app/consent gates as the rest of authenticated UI.

title: Voice output bypasses hidden-message and envelope projection defenses
description: The visual message list filters hidden system prompts via visibleMessages, and MessageBubble projects assistant envelope JSON down to .reply before rendering. The auto-TTS effect does neither: it scans raw messages for the last assistant message and calls speak(lastAiMessage.content). With voice enabled, hidden system_prompt transcript entries or leaked envelope JSON can be read aloud even though the UI intentionally suppresses them.
recommendation: Select the TTS source from the same visible/speakable message set used for rendering, exclude isSystemPrompt messages unless intentionally visible, and run assistant text through stripEnvelopeJson/formatMathContent before speak() and replay storage.

title: Dormant web ChatShell still exposes voice controls bound to stale session handlers
description: ChatShell explicitly handles RN Web keeping inactive Stack screens mounted by hiding only the input row and guarding handleSend with isFocused. Other interactive voice surfaces remain outside that dormant guard: VoicePlaybackBar, voice error retry, and VoiceTranscriptPreview still render, and handleVoiceSend calls onSend without checking focus. If an inactive session retains a pending voice transcript or voice controls overlap the active screen, a tap can send or replay content through the old session's handlers.
recommendation: Apply the same isWebDormant treatment to all interactive controls in the shell, or short-circuit every voice handler when !isFocused. Prefer wrapping the whole dormant shell in pointerEvents='none'/aria-hidden on web instead of guarding only the text input row.

title: Unescaped route and user text is rendered as trusted assistant Markdown
description: getOpeningMessage interpolates rawInput, topicName, subjectName, and recap directly into the assistant opening message. That message is later inserted as role='assistant' and rendered by MessageBubble with react-native-markdown-display, which opens Markdown links via Linking.openURL by default when no onLinkPress allowlist is supplied. A crafted app deep link or user-controlled subject/topic value such as [Continue](https://attacker.example) can therefore appear as a trusted assistant link and launch an arbitrary URL or custom scheme when tapped. This is not the scanner's crypto issue; it is a content-injection issue at the assistant/system-message trust boundary.
recommendation: Escape Markdown metacharacters in all interpolated route/user fields before building assistant messages, or render dynamic fragments as plain Text rather than Markdown. Also add a central MessageBubble onLinkPress allowlist for allowed schemes/domains and tests using rawInput/topicName values like [x](https://example.test).

title: Topic switch can double-close a session and duplicate completion side effects
description: handleTopicSwitch sets isClosing but has no synchronous in-flight guard and does not check the existing isClosing value before calling closeSession.mutateAsync({ summaryStatus: 'skipped' }). A rapid double tap on a topic before React re-renders can invoke the handler twice. The API close path uses a CAS for the row update, but for skipped summaries it still returns summaryStatus='skipped' on an already-closed session, and the route dispatches the session.completed pipeline for non-pending statuses. That can duplicate progress/XP/retention side effects for the same session.
recommendation: Add a synchronous ref lock around handleTopicSwitch, clear it only on failure/unmount, and keep the UI disabled state as secondary defense. The API close route should also avoid dispatching completion side effects when closeSession reports that the session was already closed.

title: Silence prompt timer checks stale draft text
description: scheduleSilencePrompt captures draftText when the timeout is scheduled, then checks that captured value minutes later. If the learner starts typing after the AI response, the timer still sees the old empty draft and inserts/persists the 'Still working on it?' prompt while the composer contains text. This is a timing/lifecycle bug, not a security vulnerability.
recommendation: Mirror draftText into a ref and have the timeout read the ref at fire time, or pass a current-draft getter into scheduleSilencePrompt. Add a regression test that schedules the timer, updates the draft, advances timers, and verifies no silence prompt is added.

title: Subject creation flow can write to child profiles in proxy mode
description: The subject-resolution handlers create subjects before continuing the session. Tracing useCreateSubject shows these calls POST to /subjects; the API route in apps/api/src/routes/subjects.ts calls createSubjectWithStructure with the resolved profileId but does not call assertNotProxyMode, unlike session write routes. A parent acting on a child profile can therefore add subjects/curriculum to the child profile through this flow even though the later session start/stream is proxy-guarded. Client UI checks are not an authorization boundary.
recommendation: Add server-side assertNotProxyMode to subject write routes, especially POST /subjects and related PATCH/language/retry routes, and add negative-path tests using a child X-Profile-Id with profileMeta.isOwner=false. Keep any client-side hiding as UX only.

title: LLM-backed subject classification and creation are reachable outside quota metering
description: This hook invokes classifySubject, resolveSubject, and createSubject before the metered session stream begins. The imported hooks post to /subjects/classify, /subjects/resolve, and /subjects; tracing those server paths shows routeAndCall usage in subject-classify, subject-resolve, language-detect, and book-generation, while the metering middleware only matches session, quiz, dictation, recall-bridge, and evaluate-depth LLM routes. An authenticated attacker can automate these subject endpoints to burn LLM capacity without decrementing session quota; pendingClassification and quotaError are client-side state and do not mitigate direct API abuse.
recommendation: Meter or rate-limit all subject LLM routes server-side before routeAndCall, including /subjects/classify, /subjects/resolve, and /subjects creation paths. Add abuse tests proving repeated calls consume quota or return 429/402.

title: Consent request email sending can be abused by rotating recipient addresses
description: The hook posts attacker-controlled ConsentRequest data directly to /consent/request. Tracing the API shows the route only verifies that childProfileId belongs to the authenticated account and that parentEmail differs from the account email; it does not enforce a per-account/profile/IP send limit or verify that the target profile currently requires parental consent. The service-side MAX_CONSENT_RESENDS limit is per current parentEmail and explicitly resets when parentEmail changes before calling Resend, so an authenticated attacker can keep changing recipient addresses to send arbitrary consent emails and consume email quota.
recommendation: Add a server-side rate limit for /consent/request keyed by account/profile and source IP, cap distinct recipient changes, and reject requests for profiles that do not require consent. Keep the resend counter separate from a global send budget so changing parentEmail cannot reset abuse protection.

title: OCR upload path reaches paid LLM OCR without metering
description: The mobile hook posts arbitrary selected image data to `${getApiUrl()}/v1/ocr` at line 214. `getApiUrl()` is config-derived, so the scanner's SSRF concern is a false positive, but the server route behind this call accepts authenticated multipart image uploads and invokes `GeminiOcrProvider.extractText()`, which routes through the LLM router. The API metering middleware covers several session, quiz, and dictation LLM routes but does not include `/ocr`, and the OCR route has no equivalent per-profile rate limit or quota check. An authenticated attacker can repeatedly upload up to the server-side image limit and burn paid multimodal OCR/LLM capacity outside the normal quota controls.
recommendation: Add `/v1/ocr` to the same metering/quota path as other LLM-backed operations, or introduce a dedicated OCR per-account/profile budget and rate limit before the provider call. Add a negative-path test that verifies quota exhaustion blocks OCR before any provider invocation.

title: Push token registration can write to the wrong active profile
description: The hook snapshots `activeProfileId` for local bookkeeping at line 69, but the actual registration mutation at line 129 relies on the global API client profile header at fetch time. Because token lookup and registration are asynchronous, a profile switch can make the request use a different `X-Profile-Id` than the one recorded locally. The hook is also mounted unconditionally in the app layout while parent-proxy sessions are possible, and the server push-token route does not use the proxy write guard. A parent viewing a child profile, or a rapid profile switch during registration, can overwrite the child profile's single stored Expo push token with the parent device token, causing later profile notifications to route to the wrong device and replacing the intended token.
recommendation: Skip push-token registration while in parent proxy mode and verify the active profile is still the same immediately before mutating. Pass an explicit snapshotted profile id or actor context through the request and have the server reject proxy-mode writes for push-token registration. Consider storing per-device tokens instead of one token per profile to avoid overwrites.

title: Client can persist arbitrary system-role LLM instructions
description: The hook exposes `useRecordSystemPrompt`, which sends caller-supplied `content` and `metadata` to `/v1/sessions/:sessionId/system-prompt` at lines 589-591. The traced server route validates only that `content` is a non-empty string, checks profile ownership of the session, and stores the event as `system_prompt`. Later exchange construction maps `system_prompt` events to LLM messages with role `system`; OpenAI preserves that role and Anthropic collapses all system messages into the provider-level system prompt. A tampered authenticated client can therefore inject arbitrary system-level instructions into later tutoring exchanges for its session, bypassing the intended server-owned prompt boundary and potentially manipulating safety, memory handling, or structured envelope behavior.
recommendation: Do not accept free-form system prompt text from the client. Replace this endpoint with server-owned, allowlisted prompt actions or event types whose prompt content is generated server-side, enforce strict length/type limits, and add tests proving arbitrary client text is never forwarded as an LLM system message.

title: Closed sessions can still be mutated through message APIs
description: The hook can post messages or start streams for any supplied `sessionId` at lines 286, 427, and 443. Tracing the server side shows the message and stream handlers verify profile ownership but do not require the session to still be active before generating another exchange, inserting events, incrementing `exchangeCount`, and updating `lastActivityAt`. The close path has a status transition guard, but the later message persistence path does not include a `status = active` condition. A modified client can append new messages or LLM output to completed or auto-closed sessions, corrupting transcripts, summaries, retention/export state, and session analytics.
recommendation: Enforce an active-session precondition in the message, stream, system-prompt, and related mutation services, ideally with a compare-and-set update that includes `status = active`. If resuming closed sessions is intended, route it through an explicit resume flow that creates or reopens state deliberately and is covered by tests.

title: Subject creation can trigger unmetered LLM work
description: The hook sends user-controlled subject creation input to POST /subjects. The server route is authenticated and profile-scoped, but it is not included in the metering middleware's LLM route patterns. Tracing createSubjectWithStructure shows subject creation can call detectLanguageSubject and detectSubjectType through routeAndCall, and focused-book creation can enqueue later curriculum generation. I found no subject creation rate limit or subject cap in the traced route/service path, so an authenticated attacker can loop subject creation to consume LLM/provider capacity and create database rows outside the normal visible-question quota.
recommendation: Protect LLM-backed subject creation with the same quota/metering system or a dedicated per-profile/account rate limit, add a practical subject cap or idempotency guard, and include negative tests showing repeated POST /subjects calls are throttled or charged.

title: Profile ID hashing uses non-cryptographic algorithm with public key, defeating intended anonymization
description:
The `hashProfileId()` function was designed to pseudonymize profile IDs before sending them to Sentry analytics (plan doc states: 'Pass as a stable HMAC (rotating key in Doppler) — NEVER raw, since Sentry retains tag values'). However, the implementation deviates from the design in three ways:

1. Uses a dual-FNV non-cryptographic hash (lines 53-58) instead of HMAC — FNV has known weaknesses for keyed constructions and produces only 64 bits of output.
2. The key is `EXPO_PUBLIC_ANALYTICS_HASH_KEY_V1` (line 51) — the `EXPO_PUBLIC_` prefix means it's baked into the client bundle at build time, making it trivially extractable by anyone with the app binary.
3. Falls back to a hardcoded constant `'local-analytics-key-v1'` (line 51) if the env var is unset.

Impact: If analytics data (Sentry) is breached or accessed by an unauthorized party who also has access to the user database, they can compute hashes for all known profile IDs using the public key and correlate them to Sentry events. The intended privacy boundary (making profile_id_hash non-reversible without a secret) is not achieved because the 'secret' is public. Note: UUID entropy (128 bits) prevents going from hash→profileId without the ID list, so this is primarily a bulk-correlation risk, not a direct PII exposure.
recommendation: Move the hash key to a server-side secret (Doppler, not EXPO_PUBLIC). Either compute the hash server-side before forwarding analytics events, or use a proper HMAC (e.g., HMAC-SHA256 truncated) with a key that never leaves the server. If client-side hashing is required, use a Web Crypto HMAC with a key fetched at app init and held only in memory. Remove the hardcoded fallback.

title: COPPA age threshold inconsistent with documented rounding-up compensation
description: The `calculateAge()` function (L148-149) uses year-only arithmetic (`currentYear - birthYear`) which the inline comment correctly documents as 'always rounds UP (overestimates age by up to 11 months)'. The comment prescribes using `<=` thresholds to compensate (e.g., 'age <= 16 not age < 16'). However, the COPPA-critical under-13 gate at L172 uses `age < 13` instead of `age <= 13`. This means a child born in December 2013, evaluated in May 2026, gets `calculateAge(2013) = 13`, which makes `age < 13` false — so they fall into the 13-16 bracket. If their consent status is null (consent not yet requested), the else branch at L185 enables Sentry, sending telemetry for an actual 12-year-old without parental consent. The 16-year boundary correctly uses `age <= 16` as the comment prescribes, but the 13-year boundary does not follow the same pattern.
recommendation: Change `if (age < 13)` to `if (age <= 13)` on line 172 to catch the year-rounding edge case where an actual 12-year-old gets a calculated age of 13. This matches the documented mitigation strategy in the comment above `calculateAge()` and ensures COPPA-age children with ambiguous calculated ages are protected by default. Alternatively, refactor to accept month/day (if available) for precise age calculation at this regulatory boundary.

title: Neon WebSocket pool caching is unsafe for Worker-backed background invocations
description: createDatabase() defaults to reusing a module-level NeonPool for Neon URLs unless callers pass { cacheNeonPool: false }. The file itself documents that Cloudflare Workers can reject pooled WebSocket I/O reused outside the request context, and the API request middleware mitigates this by passing cacheNeonPool: false. However, apps/api/src/inngest/helpers.ts calls createDatabase(url) without that option and caches the returned database handle across Inngest function executions. In production Workers this can reuse a Neon WebSocket pool created under an earlier request/function context, causing durable background jobs to fail or become flaky after isolate reuse. This is an outage/data-processing reliability bug rather than an attacker-exploitable security issue.
recommendation: Make the safe Worker behavior the default, or require an explicit opt-in for module-level Neon pool reuse. Update Inngest database creation to pass { cacheNeonPool: false } and close the per-invocation pool when the function finishes, or provide a Worker-safe helper that manages the pool lifecycle for Inngest separately.

title: numericAsNumber writes non-finite numbers without validation
description: The helper rejects corrupt driver strings on read, but the write path accepts any runtime number and serializes it with String(value). TypeScript's data: number type does not enforce runtime finiteness, so NaN or Infinity reaching a numericAsNumber column is emitted as "NaN" or "Infinity". PostgreSQL numeric can store NaN, and rejected special values still fail only at the database boundary. This can corrupt SRS/progress numeric columns or make later reads throw through parseNumericFromDriver. The flow is realistic because several writers compute mastery/ease values from runtime calculations and LLM-parsed output.
recommendation: Make toDriver mirror the read-side guard: require typeof value === 'number' and Number.isFinite(value) before stringifying, with the column name in the thrown error. Also validate upstream masteryScore/easeFactor calculations before persistence.

title: Recall test can create retention state for an unowned topic
description: The retention_cards schema stores profileId and topicId as independent foreign keys, so the database permits a row pairing the attacker's profile with any existing curriculum topic. The /retention/recall-test flow accepts a user-supplied topicId, calls processRecallTest(), and if the caller has no scoped retention card it auto-creates one via ensureRetentionCard(db, profileId, input.topicId) before verifying the topic belongs to the profile. The same flow then reads curriculumTopics by topicId without a profile/subject ownership join and can return the unowned topic title/description in dont_remember hints or remediation responses. An authenticated user who obtains another profile's topic UUID can therefore create cross-tenant retention rows and disclose that topic metadata.
recommendation: Before auto-creating or reading a retention card for a topicId, verify ownership through curriculumTopics -> curricula -> subjects with subjects.profileId = profileId, and return a generic 404 for unowned topics. Consider adding a materialized subjectId/profile ownership constraint or composite validation helper so retentionCards cannot pair a profile with an unrelated topic.

title: RevenueCat event ordering is enforced with a non-atomic check-then-write
description: The subscription row stores lastRevenuecatEventId and lastRevenuecatEventTimestampMs for replay/stale-event protection, but the related service path checks isRevenuecatEventProcessed() in a separate read before updating the subscription. updateSubscriptionFromRevenuecatWebhook() and activateSubscriptionFromRevenuecat() then write the new event fields without a conditional timestamp predicate. If RevenueCat delivers two events for the same account concurrently, both can pass the stale check and the older event can commit last, overwriting newer tier/status/quota state and causing billing entitlement drift.
recommendation: Make RevenueCat state transitions atomic: store the event timestamp as a numeric/bigint column and perform compare-and-swap updates with a WHERE clause that only accepts newer timestamps or the same event id. Run quota/KV side effects only when the guarded update wins, or serialize per subscription with a transaction/advisory lock.

title: DB default for learning mode conflicts with service default
description: The schema defaults learning_modes.mode to "serious", while the settings service returns "casual" when no learning_modes row exists. Several production paths create a learning_modes row for unrelated state: incrementSummarySkips() inserts without a mode and therefore relies on this DB default, and upsertCelebrationLevel() explicitly inserts mode "serious" when only changing celebration level. A new learner can therefore be treated as casual until one of these paths creates the row, after which getLearningMode() returns serious and enables mastery gates, pending XP, and mandatory summary behavior without an explicit mode change.
recommendation: Align the database default with the service default, or ensure every insert path sets mode from a single shared default. Add a regression test that creating a learning_modes row via summary-skip or celebration-level paths preserves the intended default mode.

title: Curriculum topic book ownership is not constrained, enabling cross-profile topic moves
description: curriculum_topics stores curriculumId and bookId as independent foreign keys, but the schema does not enforce that the referenced book belongs to the same subject/profile as the referenced curriculum. This becomes exploitable in the production moveTopicToBook() flow: apps/api/src/services/curriculum.ts verifies the caller owns the route subject and targetBookId, but it only checks that the supplied topicId currently has the supplied source bookId, then updates curriculum_topics by topic id. It never verifies that the source book or topic belong to the caller's subject/profile. An authenticated user who obtains another profile's topic and book UUIDs can reassign that other learner's topic row to the attacker's book, corrupting cross-tenant curriculum state.
recommendation: Fix moveTopicToBook() to verify source book, target book, topic, and curriculum through the same caller-owned subject/profile chain before updating, and include the verified source book/curriculum predicates in the UPDATE where clause. Consider adding a DB-level invariant, such as carrying subjectId on curriculum_topics with composite foreign keys or a trigger, so bookId and curriculumId cannot point across different subjects.

title: GC1 mock guard misses multiline jest.mock calls
description:
The detector only applies MOCK_LINE to each added diff line independently, so a new internal mock written as a multiline call such as jest.mock(
  './services/foo',
  () => ({ ... })
) has no single added line matching the regex and is not checked for Pattern A or gc1-allow. This weakens the CI/pre-commit ratchet and can allow broad internal mocks to land despite the stated guard. Prettier will collapse simple cases, but long or commented call expressions can remain multiline.
recommendation: Parse staged/HEAD test files with the TypeScript AST and inspect jest.mock/jest.doMock call expressions whose start line was added, or assemble multiline added hunks before matching.

title: CSV output can contain spreadsheet formulas from mock targets
description: The script extracts literal jest/vi mock targets from repository test files and writes them directly into a committed CSV. `target` is populated from the first string-literal mock argument, then `renderCsv()` emits it through `csvCell()`. `csvCell()` only handles CSV quoting for commas, quotes, and newlines; it does not neutralize cells beginning with spreadsheet formula characters such as `=`, `+`, `-`, or `@`. A malicious contributor could add a mock target that is inert TypeScript but becomes a formula if a maintainer opens `docs/plans/2026-05-12-internal-mock-cleanup-inventory.csv` in Excel or Google Sheets, potentially causing network callbacks or local data disclosure through spreadsheet formula behavior.
recommendation: When rendering CSV intended for human spreadsheet use, prefix formula-like cell values with a single quote or another accepted neutralization strategy before CSV quoting. Apply this to all string cells, especially `target`, `file`, and free-text reason fields.

title: Diff mode ignores changed source strings for existing i18n keys
description: Non-full translation mode calls getMissingAndExtraKeys(), which compares only key presence. If an existing en.json key changes value but keeps the same key, missingKeys and extraKeys are both empty, the script logs 'No changes detected' and skips that locale. The CI i18n staleness guard checks keys and interpolation variables, not whether translated text reflects changed source copy, so stale user-facing translations can ship silently.
recommendation: Reuse computeChangedKeys() from scripts/translate.ts or compare flattened source values as well as key presence, then translate changed and added keys, prune removed keys, and validate the merged output.
